For settings where the corresponding offline problem is NP-hard, it is necessary to allow a multiplicative approximation factor.
our result is especially interesting in this context, since we achieve γ \gamma -regret that scales as O ​ ( log 3 ⁡ T ) O(\log^{3}T) while prior frameworks including those due to Kakade et al. [ 32 ] and Niazadeh et al. [ 42 ] have γ \gamma -regret which scales as T 2 / 3 T^{2/3} . The exploration strategies specified by many standard approaches to bandit problems can be computationally intractable for large action spaces [ 36 ] . As a result, there is significant interest in developing algorithms with more efficient implementations [ 41 , 48 , 50 , 43 , 18 , 7 ] .
Since our algorithm is based on local search and performs exploration locally, it naturally lends itself to a computationally efficient implementation.

sections.heading: 1.3 Roadmap
sections.body:
We organize the rest of this paper as follows. Section ˜ 2 formally sets up our model and recalls preliminary results we need for our analysis.
Then we describe our ‘offline-to-online’ algorithm utilizing local search and provide a high-level overview of its analysis in Section ˜ 3 .
Following this, Section ˜ 4 demonstrates the applicability of our framework on the problems we discussed above. Section ˜ 5 concludes the paper and discusses potential directions for future work.

sections.heading: 2 Preliminaries
sections.body:
We now formally define the general problem setting we consider — stochastic combinatorial bandits with local search.
First, each instance is associated with a set 𝒳 \mathcal{X} of feasible solutions 1 1 1 We are mainly interested in the case where 𝒳 \mathcal{X} is finite (but potentially large), so minimizing a function over 𝒳 \mathcal{X} is well-defined. and for each x ∈ 𝒳 x\in\mathcal{X} there is a random non-negative cost associated with it.
We model the randomness as a collection of latent variables taking values in some space 𝒵 \mathcal{Z} , for which there is an unknown distribution 𝒟 ∈ Δ ​ ( 𝒵 ) \mathcal{D}\in\Delta(\mathcal{Z}) over the possible values the latent variables can take.
Thus we can model the cost as a function 𝖢𝗈𝗌𝗍 : 𝒳 × 𝒵 → ℝ + \operatorname{{\sf Cost}}:\mathcal{X}\times\mathcal{Z}\to\mathbb{R}_{+} , which induces a distribution on the realized cost for a fixed x ∈ 𝒳 x\in\mathcal{X} as 𝖢𝗈𝗌𝗍 ⁡ ( x , Z ) \operatorname{{\sf Cost}}(x,Z) , where Z ∼ 𝒟 Z\sim\mathcal{D} .
To streamline our notation, we let 𝖢𝗈𝗌𝗍 ⁡ ( x ) := 𝔼 Z ∼ 𝒟 ​ [ 𝖢𝗈𝗌𝗍 ⁡ ( x , Z ) ] \operatorname{{\sf Cost}}(x):=\mathbb{E}_{Z\sim\mathcal{D}}[\operatorname{{\sf Cost}}(x,Z)] be the expected cost of playing x ∈ 𝒳 x\in\mathcal{X} and set OPT := min x ∈ 𝒳 ⁡ 𝖢𝗈𝗌𝗍 ⁡ ( x ) \mathrm{OPT}:=\min_{x\in\mathcal{X}}\operatorname{{\sf Cost}}(x) to be the minimum expected cost of any feasible solution. In the online bandit setting, we consider making a sequence of decisions { x t } t = 1 T \{x_{t}\}_{t=1}^{T} , where each x t ∈ 𝒳 x_{t}\in\mathcal{X} , in order to optimize an objective over a time horizon of T T periods in the presence of bandit (cost) feedback.
An algorithm (or policy) 𝒜 \mathcal{A} is a sequence of maps { 𝒜 t : ℋ t − 1 → 𝒳 } t = 1 T \{\mathcal{A}_{t}:\mathcal{H}_{t-1}\to\mathcal{X}\}_{t=1}^{T} which each takes the observed history H t − 1 H^{t-1} up to period t − 1 t-1 and returns a new solution x t x_{t} to be used in period t t 2 2 2 We focus on deterministic algorithms. We can extend to randomized algorithms by letting A t A_{t} be a map to Δ ​ ( 𝒳 ) \Delta(\mathcal{X}) and taking an additional expectation over the realized distribution of actions. .
In the bandit setting, a history up to period t t is a sequence { X s , Y s } s = 1 t \{X_{s},Y_{s}\}_{s=1}^{t} , where Y s = 𝖢𝗈𝗌𝗍 ⁡ ( X s , Z s ) Y_{s}=\operatorname{{\sf Cost}}(X_{s},Z_{s}) is the observed random cost at step s s for some independent sample Z s ∼ 𝒟 Z_{s}\sim\mathcal{D} .
Notice { Z s } s = 1 T \{Z_{s}\}_{s=1}^{T} , the realizations of randomness that underlying the cost or reward, is unobserved.
Formally, histories are random variables over ( 𝒳 × ℝ + ) t (\mathcal{X}\times\mathbb{R}_{+})^{t} .
We let ℋ t = ( 𝒳 × ℝ + ) t \mathcal{H}^{t}=(\mathcal{X}\times\mathbb{R}_{+})^{t} be the set of all length t t sequences over solution-cost pairs.
For simplicity, we let 𝒜 1 \mathcal{A}_{1} be a constant function since no history has been observed at period 1.
Our main interest is giving algorithms with low γ \gamma -regret, which measures the difference in cumulative expected cost between the algorithm and using a γ \gamma -approximate solution for all T T periods, which we can now define formally. For an algorithm 𝒜 \mathcal{A} , latent distribution 𝒟 \mathcal{D} , time horizon T T , and γ ≥ 1 \gamma\geq 1 , we define to be the T T -period γ \gamma -regret of algorithm 𝒜 \mathcal{A} on distribution 𝒟 \mathcal{D} , where the expectation is taken with respect to the independent samples ( Z 1 , Z 2 , … , Z T ) ∼ 𝒟 T (Z_{1},Z_{2},\ldots,Z_{T})\sim\mathcal{D}^{T} and any internal randomness utilized by 𝒜 \mathcal{A} .
We say that algorithm 𝒜 \mathcal{A} has γ \gamma -regret R ​ ( T ) R(T) if for all distributions 𝒟 \mathcal{D} , 𝖱𝖾𝗀𝗋𝖾𝗍 γ ⁡ ( 𝒜 , 𝒟 , T ) ≤ R ​ ( T ) \operatorname{{\sf Regret}}_{\gamma}(\mathcal{A},\mathcal{D},T)\leq R(T) . We remark that if γ = 1 \gamma=1 , then we recover the standard definition of regret used in stochastic multi-armed bandits.
As discussed in Section ˜ 1.1 , our goal is to find algorithms with γ \gamma -regret whose dependence on T T is not just o ​ ( T ) o(T) but is O ​ ( poly ⁡ ( log ⁡ T ) ) O(\operatorname{poly}(\log T)) , which is o ​ ( T ρ ) o(T^{\rho}) for any ρ ∈ ( 0 , 1 ) \rho\in(0,1) . Local search will allow us to avoid some of the difficulties that arise with exploring the large action spaces that can arise in stochastic combinatorial bandit problems.
For a feasible solution space 𝒳 \mathcal{X} , let 𝒩 : 𝒳 → 2 𝒳 \mathcal{N}:\mathcal{X}\to 2^{\mathcal{X}} be its neighborhood map so that for each x ∈ 𝒳 x\in\mathcal{X} , 𝒩 ​ ( x ) \mathcal{N}(x) is a set of neighboring solutions to x x .
We assume that M := max x ∈ 𝒳 ⁡ | 𝒩 ​ ( x ) | M:=\max_{x\in\mathcal{X}}|\mathcal{N}(x)| is a priori known to the algorithm, which holds in all the examples we consider.
The key property of a neighborhood map that we require to achieve strong γ \gamma -regret bounds is that any solution which has expected cost worse than γ ​ OPT \gamma\mathrm{OPT} has a neighboring solution which has multiplicative improvement to its cost. Consider the problem specified by feasible set 𝒳 \mathcal{X} , neighborhood map 𝒩 \mathcal{N} , and expected cost function 𝖢𝗈𝗌𝗍 : 𝒳 → ℝ + \operatorname{{\sf Cost}}:\mathcal{X}\to\mathbb{R}_{+} as defined above.
We say the problem admits ( β , γ ) (\beta,\gamma) -improving moves if, for any x ∈ 𝒳 x\in\mathcal{X} with 𝖢𝗈𝗌𝗍 ⁡ ( x ) > γ ​ OPT \operatorname{{\sf Cost}}(x)>\gamma\mathrm{OPT} , there exists x ′ ∈ 𝒩 ​ ( x ) x^{\prime}\in\mathcal{N}(x) with 𝖢𝗈𝗌𝗍 ⁡ ( x ′ ) ≤ β ​ 𝖢𝗈𝗌𝗍 ⁡ ( x ) \operatorname{{\sf Cost}}(x^{\prime})\leq\beta\operatorname{{\sf Cost}}(x) . The parameters β \beta and γ \gamma can depend on the structure of the problem (e.g., the number of jobs in completion time scheduling) and β \beta can depend on γ \gamma (e.g., if γ = 1 + ϵ \gamma=1+\epsilon , then β \beta can depend on ϵ \epsilon ). A priori, it is perhaps unclear how to establish whether a problem permits ( β , γ ) (\beta,\gamma) -improving moves. Fortunately, there is a straightforward correspondence showing that all problems where Algorithm ˜ 1 terminates in an approximate solution must permit ( β , γ ) (\beta,\gamma) -improving moves. Given instance ( 𝒳 , 𝒩 , 𝖢𝗈𝗌𝗍 ) (\mathcal{X},\mathcal{N},\operatorname{{\sf Cost}}) , whenever Algorithm ˜ 1 is guaranteed to terminate in a γ \gamma -approximation for some choice of β \beta , then ( 𝒳 , 𝒩 , 𝖢𝗈𝗌𝗍 ) (\mathcal{X},\mathcal{N},\operatorname{{\sf Cost}}) permits ( β , γ ) (\beta,\gamma) -improving moves. We proceed by contradiction. Suppose that Algorithm ˜ 1 with parameter β \beta terminates in a γ \gamma -approximation on a problem defined by ( 𝒳 , 𝒩 , 𝖢𝗈𝗌𝗍 ) (\mathcal{X},\mathcal{N},\operatorname{{\sf Cost}}) , but the problem does not permit ( β , γ ) (\beta,\gamma) improving moves. Then there exists solution x ∈ 𝒳 x\in\mathcal{X} such that 𝖢𝗈𝗌𝗍 ⁡ ( x ) > γ ​ OPT \operatorname{{\sf Cost}}(x)>\gamma\mathrm{OPT} and 𝖢𝗈𝗌𝗍 ⁡ ( x ′ ) > β ​ 𝖢𝗈𝗌𝗍 ⁡ ( x ) \operatorname{{\sf Cost}}(x^{\prime})>\beta\operatorname{{\sf Cost}}(x) for all x ′ ∈ 𝒩 ​ ( x ) x^{\prime}\in\mathcal{N}(x) . However, when using x x as the starting x 0 x_{0} in Algorithm ˜ 1 , the algorithm converges in some solution x ∘ x^{\circ} with 𝖢𝗈𝗌𝗍 ⁡ ( x ∘ ) ≤ γ ​ OPT \operatorname{{\sf Cost}}(x^{\circ})\leq\gamma\mathrm{OPT} .
Now either x = x ∘ x=x^{\circ} , or the algorithm proceeds to a subsequent solution which yields a β \beta -factor improvement en route to x ∘ x^{\circ} . In either case, this contradicts ( 𝒳 , 𝒩 , 𝖢𝗈𝗌𝗍 ) (\mathcal{X},\mathcal{N},\operatorname{{\sf Cost}}) not permitting ( β , γ ) (\beta,\gamma) -improving moves.
∎ Finally, we require the following version of the Chernoff bounds, see, e.g., [ 20 ] for a reference. Suppose that X 1 , X 2 , … , X N X_{1},X_{2},\ldots,X_{N} are independent random variables in the interval [ 0 , 1 ] [0,1] . Let μ = 1 N ​ ∑ i 𝔼 ​ [ X i ] \mu=\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i}\mathbb{E}[X_{i}] and X ¯ = 1 N ​ ∑ s X s \bar{X}=\frac{1}{N}\sum_{s}X_{s} , then for all δ ∈ ( 0 , 1 ) \delta\in(0,1) and any μ H , μ L \mu_{H},\mu_{L} such that μ ∈ [ μ L , μ H ] \mu\in[\mu_{L},\mu_{H}] we have:

sections.heading: 3 Stochastic Combinatorial Bandits from Offline Local Search
sections.body:
Our algorithm is formally described in Algorithms ˜ 2 and 3 .
The algorithm operates in a sequence of phases ℓ = 1 , 2 , … \ell=1,2,\ldots which are managed by the “for” loop in Algorithm ˜ 2 .
Each phase keeps track of a solution x ℓ x_{\ell} and a cost threshold θ ℓ := α ℓ − 1 ​ C max = β ( ℓ − 1 ) / 2 ​ C max \theta_{\ell}:=\alpha^{\ell-1}C_{\max}=\beta^{(\ell-1)/2}C_{\max} .
The invariant which, due to stochasticity, we wish to maintain with high probability is that 𝖢𝗈𝗌𝗍 ⁡ ( x ℓ ) ≤ θ ℓ \operatorname{{\sf Cost}}(x_{\ell})\leq\theta_{\ell} .
Thus, assuming 𝖢𝗈𝗌𝗍 ⁡ ( x ℓ ) ≤ θ ℓ \operatorname{{\sf Cost}}(x_{\ell})\leq\theta_{\ell} , in phase ℓ \ell we wish to either determine that 𝖢𝗈𝗌𝗍 ⁡ ( x ℓ ) ≤ α ​ θ ℓ = θ ℓ + 1 \operatorname{{\sf Cost}}(x_{\ell})\leq\alpha\theta_{\ell}=\theta_{\ell+1} , find some x ′ ∈ 𝒩 ​ ( x ℓ ) x^{\prime}\in\mathcal{N}(x_{\ell}) with 𝖢𝗈𝗌𝗍 ⁡ ( x ′ ) ≤ α ​ 𝖢𝗈𝗌𝗍 ⁡ ( x ℓ ) ≤ α ​ θ ℓ = θ ℓ + 1 \operatorname{{\sf Cost}}(x^{\prime})\leq\alpha\operatorname{{\sf Cost}}(x_{\ell})\leq\alpha\theta_{\ell}=\theta_{\ell+1} , or determine that no such x ′ x^{\prime} exists.
In the first case we may set x ℓ + 1 = x ℓ x_{\ell+1}=x_{\ell} to maintain the invariant, while in the second we may set x ℓ + 1 = x ′ x_{\ell+1}=x^{\prime} to maintain the invariant.
In the last case, we will use the fact that 𝒩 \mathcal{N} satisfies Definition ˜ 2.2 and no x ′ x^{\prime} was found to conclude that 𝖢𝗈𝗌𝗍 ⁡ ( x ℓ ) ≤ γ ​ OPT \operatorname{{\sf Cost}}(x_{\ell})\leq\gamma\mathrm{OPT} , and thus we use the current solution x ℓ x_{\ell} for all remaining periods. To achieve this, Algorithm ˜ 2 first uses solution x ℓ x_{\ell} for N ℓ N_{\ell} time steps to get a rough estimate of its cost, then compares it to a threshold.
The threshold is set so that if 𝖢𝗈𝗌𝗍 ⁡ ( x ℓ ) ≤ α 2 ​ θ ℓ \operatorname{{\sf Cost}}(x_{\ell})\leq\alpha^{2}\theta_{\ell} then the estimated cost is highly likely to be smaller than the threshold.
As discussed above, we then move to phase ℓ + 1 \ell+1 with x ℓ + 1 = x ℓ x_{\ell+1}=x_{\ell} .
If instead, the estimated cost is larger than the threshold, we run the subroutine in Algorithm ˜ 3 which explores the neighborhood of x ℓ x_{\ell} .
This subroutine returns a solution x 𝖭𝖾𝗐 x_{\operatorname{{\sf New}}} , which is either some x ′ ∈ 𝒩 ​ ( x ) x^{\prime}\in\mathcal{N}(x) or x ℓ x_{\ell} .
If x 𝖭𝖾𝗐 x_{\operatorname{{\sf New}}} is some x ′ ∈ 𝒩 ​ ( x ) x^{\prime}\in\mathcal{N}(x) , then we set x ℓ + 1 = x 𝖭𝖾𝗐 x_{\ell+1}=x_{\operatorname{{\sf New}}} and move to phase ℓ + 1 \ell+1 .
Critically, we will show that with high probability 𝖢𝗈𝗌𝗍 ⁡ ( x 𝖭𝖾𝗐 ) ≤ α ​ θ ℓ = θ ℓ + 1 \operatorname{{\sf Cost}}(x_{\operatorname{{\sf New}}})\leq\alpha\theta_{\ell}=\theta_{\ell+1} to maintain the invariant in this case.
Finally if x 𝖭𝖾𝗐 = x ℓ x_{\operatorname{{\sf New}}}=x_{\ell} , this indicates that nothing significantly better was found and so we break out of the “for” loop over phase ℓ \ell , setting x 𝖫𝖺𝗌𝗍 = x ℓ x_{\operatorname{{\sf Last}}}=x_{\ell} and using x 𝖫𝖺𝗌𝗍 x_{\operatorname{{\sf Last}}} until period T T since, by Definition ˜ 2.2 , x 𝖫𝖺𝗌𝗍 x_{\operatorname{{\sf Last}}} will satisfy 𝖢𝗈𝗌𝗍 ⁡ ( x 𝖫𝖺𝗌𝗍 ) ≤ γ ​ OPT \operatorname{{\sf Cost}}(x_{\operatorname{{\sf Last}}})\leq\gamma\mathrm{OPT} with high probability. As discussed, we want Algorithm ˜ 3 to either find a neighboring solution which improves upon x ℓ x_{\ell} by at least an α \alpha -factor or indicate that no such improvement exists in 𝒩 ​ ( x ℓ ) \mathcal{N}(x_{\ell}) , and for this to hold with high probability.
It is thus inevitable that a significant number of time steps are spent exploring the neighbourhood of x ℓ x_{\ell} . Doing so without incurring high regret requires some care.
A first attempt might involve sampling each neighboring solution N ℓ N_{\ell} times, as we did for x ℓ x_{\ell} in Algorithm ˜ 2 , and comparing to an appropriate threshold.
This may potentially incur linear regret in later phases when a neighboring solution could be significantly worse than the current solution x ℓ x_{\ell} .
To avoid this, we sample neighboring solutions cautiously over a series of “sub-phases” of increasing length ( Algorithm ˜ 3 , line 4), where we progressively increase the amount of sampling only while the neighbouring solution has the potential to improve over x ℓ x_{\ell} .
As a result, poor neighbours are eliminated in early subphases,
reminiscent of the successive-elimination algorithm for stochastic multi-armed bandits [ 22 ] , and sampled geometrically fewer times than the best solutions (and x ℓ x_{\ell} ). Let 𝒜 𝖫𝗈𝖼𝖺𝗅 \mathcal{A}_{\sf Local} denote the algorithm described by Algorithms ˜ 2 and 3 .
Our main result is that the regret of 𝒜 𝖫𝗈𝖼𝖺𝗅 \mathcal{A}_{\sf Local} scales poly-logarithmically with T T as long as the underlying problem admits ( β , γ ) (\beta,\gamma) -improving moves. Suppose that 𝒳 \mathcal{X} , 𝒩 \mathcal{N} , and the cost function 𝖢𝗈𝗌𝗍 ⁡ ( ⋅ ) = 𝔼 z ∼ 𝒟 ​ [ 𝖢𝗈𝗌𝗍 ⁡ ( ⋅ , z ) ] \operatorname{{\sf Cost}}(\cdot)=\mathbb{E}_{z\sim\mathcal{D}}[\operatorname{{\sf Cost}}(\cdot,z)] induced by 𝒟 \mathcal{D} admit ( β , γ ) (\beta,\gamma) -improving moves.
Then for all distributions 𝒟 \mathcal{D} and all sufficiently large T T , we have where 𝒜 𝖫𝗈𝖼𝖺𝗅 \mathcal{A}_{\sf Local} is given by Algorithms ˜ 2 and 3 , α = β \alpha=\sqrt{\beta} , C max = max x ∈ 𝒳 , z ∈ 𝒵 ⁡ 𝖢𝗈𝗌𝗍 ⁡ ( x , z ) C_{\max}=\max_{x\in\mathcal{X},z\in\mathcal{Z}}\operatorname{{\sf Cost}}(x,z) , and δ = 1 − α 1 + α \delta=\frac{1-\alpha}{1+\alpha} . We now perform the analysis which will yield Theorem ˜ 3.1 .
First, we encapsulate our applications of concentration inequalities into the following lemma which we will apply several times. Fix a solution x ∈ 𝒳 x\in\mathcal{X} and let z 1 , z 2 , … , z N z_{1},z_{2},\ldots,z_{N} be N := 3 ​ C max ​ ( 4 ​ log ⁡ T + log ⁡ M ) / ( δ 2 ​ α 2 ​ θ ) N:=3C_{\max}(4\log T+\log M)/(\delta^{2}\alpha^{2}\theta) independent samples from 𝒟 \mathcal{D} , where ℓ , M ∈ ℕ \ell,M\in\mathbb{N} , θ > 0 \theta>0 , α ∈ ( 0 , 1 ) \alpha\in(0,1) , and δ = 1 − α 1 + α ∈ ( 0 , 1 ) \delta=\frac{1-\alpha}{1+\alpha}\in(0,1) .
Let 𝖢𝗈𝗌𝗍 ^ ​ ( x ) = 1 N ​ ∑ s = 1 N 𝖢𝗈𝗌𝗍 ⁡ ( x , z s ) \widehat{\operatorname{{\sf Cost}}}(x)=\frac{1}{N}\sum_{s=1}^{N}\operatorname{{\sf Cost}}(x,z_{s}) . Then (a) 𝖢𝗈𝗌𝗍 ⁡ ( x ) ≤ α 2 ​ θ ⟹ Pr z 1 , z 2 , … , z N ∼ 𝒟 N ⁡ [ 𝖢𝗈𝗌𝗍 ^ ​ ( x ) > 2 ​ α 2 1 + α ​ θ ] ≤ M − 1 ​ T − 4 \operatorname{{\sf Cost}}(x)\leq\alpha^{2}\theta\implies\Pr_{z_{1},z_{2},\ldots,z_{N}\sim\mathcal{D}^{N}}\left[\widehat{\operatorname{{\sf Cost}}}(x)>\frac{2\alpha^{2}}{1+\alpha}\theta\right]\leq M^{-1}T^{-4} , and 𝖢𝗈𝗌𝗍 ⁡ ( x ) ≤ α 2 ​ θ ⟹ Pr z 1 , z 2 , … , z N ∼ 𝒟 N ⁡ [ 𝖢𝗈𝗌𝗍 ^ ​ ( x ) > 2 ​ α 2 1 + α ​ θ ] ≤ M − 1 ​ T − 4 \operatorname{{\sf Cost}}(x)\leq\alpha^{2}\theta\implies\Pr_{z_{1},z_{2},\ldots,z_{N}\sim\mathcal{D}^{N}}\left[\widehat{\operatorname{{\sf Cost}}}(x)>\frac{2\alpha^{2}}{1+\alpha}\theta\right]\leq M^{-1}T^{-4} , and (b) 𝖢𝗈𝗌𝗍 ⁡ ( x ) ≥ α ​ θ ⟹ Pr z 1 , z 2 , … , z N ∼ 𝒟 N ⁡ [ 𝖢𝗈𝗌𝗍 ^ ​ ( x ) ≤ 2 ​ α 2 1 + α ​ θ ] ≤ M − 1 ​ T − 4 \operatorname{{\sf Cost}}(x)\geq\alpha\theta\implies\Pr_{z_{1},z_{2},\ldots,z_{N}\sim\mathcal{D}^{N}}\left[\widehat{\operatorname{{\sf Cost}}}(x)\leq\frac{2\alpha^{2}}{1+\alpha}\theta\right]\leq M^{-1}T^{-4} . 𝖢𝗈𝗌𝗍 ⁡ ( x ) ≥ α ​ θ ⟹ Pr z 1 , z 2 , … , z N ∼ 𝒟 N ⁡ [ 𝖢𝗈𝗌𝗍 ^ ​ ( x ) ≤ 2 ​ α 2 1 + α ​ θ ] ≤ M − 1 ​ T − 4 \operatorname{{\sf Cost}}(x)\geq\alpha\theta\implies\Pr_{z_{1},z_{2},\ldots,z_{N}\sim\mathcal{D}^{N}}\left[\widehat{\operatorname{{\sf Cost}}}(x)\leq\frac{2\alpha^{2}}{1+\alpha}\theta\right]\leq M^{-1}T^{-4} . For brevity, we write Pr ⁡ [ ⋅ ] := Pr z 1 , z 2 , … , z N ∼ 𝒟 N ⁡ [ ⋅ ] \Pr[\cdot]:=\Pr_{z_{1},z_{2},\ldots,z_{N}\sim\mathcal{D}^{N}}[\cdot] .
Setting X s = 𝖢𝗈𝗌𝗍 ⁡ ( x , z s ) / C max X_{s}=\operatorname{{\sf Cost}}(x,z_{s})/C_{\max} for all s ∈ [ N ] s\in[N] , we have that X s ∈ [ 0 , 1 ] X_{s}\in[0,1] and 𝔼 ​ [ X s ] = 𝖢𝗈𝗌𝗍 ⁡ ( x ) / C max \mathbb{E}[X_{s}]=\operatorname{{\sf Cost}}(x)/C_{\max} .
Observe that 2 ​ α 2 1 + α = ( 1 + δ ) ​ α 2 = ( 1 − δ ) ​ α \frac{2\alpha^{2}}{1+\alpha}=(1+\delta)\alpha^{2}=(1-\delta)\alpha for our choice of δ \delta .
For part (a), when 𝖢𝗈𝗌𝗍 ⁡ ( x ) ≤ α 2 ​ θ \operatorname{{\sf Cost}}(x)\leq\alpha^{2}\theta , observe that Since X s ∈ [ 0 , 1 ] X_{s}\in[0,1] and 𝔼 ​ [ X s ] ≤ α 2 ​ θ / C max \mathbb{E}[X_{s}]\leq\alpha^{2}\theta/C_{\max} , we may bound the right hand side using Theorem ˜ 2.3 as where the right hand side follows from our choice of N N . For part (b), when 𝖢𝗈𝗌𝗍 ⁡ ( x ) ≥ α ​ θ \operatorname{{\sf Cost}}(x)\geq\alpha\theta , it follows that Again, since X s ∈ [ 0 , 1 ] X_{s}\in[0,1] and 𝔼 ​ [ X s ] ≥ α ​ θ / C max \mathbb{E}[X_{s}]\geq\alpha\theta/C_{\max} , we bound the right hand side using Theorem ˜ 2.3 as where the right hand side follows from our choice of N N and α ≤ 1 \alpha\leq 1 . This completes the proof.
∎ Let L L be the index of the (random) last phase that is encountered in Algorithm ˜ 2 .
To bound the overall regret, we will bound the total regret in phases with index ℓ < L \ell<L and also show that the regret in the last phase L L is negligible.
Before we can do that, we need to define an appropriate sequence of “bad events” which are exceedingly unlikely, for which will be able to bound the regret when conditioning on their negation, as is standard. For each phase ℓ \ell , let ℳ ℓ \mathcal{M}_{\ell} be the event that Algorithm ˜ 2 makes it to phase ℓ \ell and let 𝒞 ℓ \mathcal{C}_{\ell} be the event that 𝖢𝗈𝗌𝗍 ⁡ ( x ℓ ) > θ ℓ \operatorname{{\sf Cost}}(x_{\ell})>\theta_{\ell} .
Then we define the bad event in phase ℓ \ell as ℬ ℓ := ℳ ℓ ∧ 𝒞 ℓ \mathcal{B}_{\ell}:=\mathcal{M}_{\ell}\wedge\mathcal{C}_{\ell} and 𝒢 ℓ = ¬ ℬ ℓ \mathcal{G}_{\ell}=\neg\mathcal{B}_{\ell} . We will show that these events are unlikely.
Intuitively, this will follow inductively by assuming that ℬ ℓ \mathcal{B}_{\ell} is unlikely, and then we can use the definition of our algorithm to bound Pr ⁡ [ ℬ ℓ + 1 ∣ 𝒢 ℓ ] \Pr[\mathcal{B}_{\ell+1}\mid\mathcal{G}_{\ell}] and show that ℬ ℓ + 1 \mathcal{B}_{\ell+1} is unlikely via a standard decomposition.
The key step involves bounding Pr ⁡ [ ℬ ℓ + 1 ∣ 𝒢 ℓ ] \Pr[\mathcal{B}_{\ell+1}\mid\mathcal{G}_{\ell}] . For each ℓ \ell , we have Pr ⁡ [ ℬ ℓ + 1 ∣ 𝒢 ℓ ] ≤ T − 3 \Pr[\mathcal{B}_{\ell+1}\mid\mathcal{G}_{\ell}]\leq T^{-3} Proving this lemma requires careful analysis of the TestNeighborhood subroutine ( Algorithm ˜ 3 ), which we postpone momentarily.
For now, we show Lemma ˜ 3.4 implies a bound on Pr ⁡ [ ℬ ℓ ] \Pr[\mathcal{B}_{\ell}] . For each ℓ \ell , we have Pr ⁡ [ ℬ ℓ ] ≤ ℓ ​ T − 3 \Pr[\mathcal{B}_{\ell}]\leq\ell T^{-3} . We proceed by induction on ℓ \ell .
For the base case when ℓ = 1 \ell=1 , we have that 𝖢𝗈𝗌𝗍 ⁡ ( x 1 ) ≤ C max ≤ θ 1 \operatorname{{\sf Cost}}(x_{1})\leq C_{\max}\leq\theta_{1} , and so Pr ⁡ [ ℬ 1 ] = 0 ≤ T − 3 \Pr[\mathcal{B}_{1}]=0\leq T^{-3} , proving the base case. Inductively, we assume that Pr ⁡ [ ℬ ℓ ] ≤ ℓ ​ T − 3 \Pr[\mathcal{B}_{\ell}]\leq\ell T^{-3} and aim to bound Pr ⁡ [ ℬ ℓ + 1 ] \Pr[\mathcal{B}_{\ell+1}] .
By the law of total probability we have completing the proof of the inductive case and giving the corollary.
∎ We now turn to analyzing Algorithm ˜ 3 .

Let R ℓ , 1 R_{\ell,1} and R ℓ , 2 R_{\ell,2} represent each of these quantities, respectively.
We start by bounding R ℓ , 1 R_{\ell,1} .
Since OPT ≥ 0 \mathrm{OPT}\geq 0 , by the law of total expectation we have: The first inequality follows since 𝖢𝗈𝗌𝗍 ⁡ ( x ℓ ) ≤ θ ℓ \operatorname{{\sf Cost}}(x_{\ell})\leq\theta_{\ell} under ¬ ℬ ℓ \neg\mathcal{B}_{\ell} (giving the first term) and Corollary ˜ 3.5 (which bounds Pr [ ℬ ℓ ] ) \Pr[\mathcal{B}_{\ell}]) .
The second inequality follows from the definition of N ℓ N_{\ell} and the fact that N ℓ N_{\ell} and ℓ \ell are both smaller than T T . Bounding R ℓ , 2 R_{\ell,2} is similar, but we have to account for each sub-phase.
We see that: The first term in the first inequality follows since in the worst case we play each neighboring solution in all sub-phases, but under event ¬ ℬ ℓ ′ \neg\mathcal{B}^{\prime}_{\ell} we never play a solution with cost more than θ ℓ ′ \theta_{\ell^{\prime}} in each sub-phase ℓ ′ \ell^{\prime} .
The second term follows since, under ℬ ℓ ′ \mathcal{B}^{\prime}_{\ell} , we can’t play a solution of cost more than C max C_{\max} for more than T T steps and Pr ⁡ [ ℬ ℓ ′ ] ≤ T − 4 \Pr[\mathcal{B}^{\prime}_{\ell}]\leq T^{-4} by Lemma ˜ 3.8 . Let R > L R_{>L} denote the regret incurred after phase L L .
We need to consider both the case where Algorithm ˜ 2 terminated with a solution x 𝖫𝖺𝗌𝗍 x_{\operatorname{{\sf Last}}} and when it did not.
Denote the former event by ℒ \mathcal{L} .
Then we have We now focus on each conditional expectation and show that the regret is small in each. Consider the first case where we condition on ℒ \mathcal{L} , and so we have that Algorithm ˜ 2 terminated with some solution x 𝖫𝖺𝗌𝗍 x_{\operatorname{{\sf Last}}} which is used for all remaining rounds.
This happens when Algorithm ˜ 3 is run in phase L L and returns the solution x L x_{L} .
We want to show that it is unlikely for x L x_{L} to satisfy 𝖢𝗈𝗌𝗍 ⁡ ( x L ) > γ ​ OPT \operatorname{{\sf Cost}}(x_{L})>\gamma\mathrm{OPT} .
Suppose 𝖢𝗈𝗌𝗍 ⁡ ( x L ) > γ ​ OPT \operatorname{{\sf Cost}}(x_{L})>\gamma\mathrm{OPT} , then by Definition ˜ 2.2 , we have that 𝒩 ​ ( x ℓ ) \mathcal{N}(x_{\ell}) contains a solution x ′ x^{\prime} with 𝖢𝗈𝗌𝗍 ⁡ ( x ′ ) ≤ β ​ 𝖢𝗈𝗌𝗍 ⁡ ( x L ) = α 2 ​ 𝖢𝗈𝗌𝗍 ⁡ ( x L ) \operatorname{{\sf Cost}}(x^{\prime})\leq\beta\operatorname{{\sf Cost}}(x_{L})=\alpha^{2}\operatorname{{\sf Cost}}(x_{L}) .
This means that event ℱ ℓ , 2 \mathcal{F}_{\ell,2} has occurred, and by Lemma ˜ 3.9 the probability of Algorithm ˜ 3 yielding x L x_{L} as output is at most ( ℓ + 1 ) ​ T − 4 ≤ T − 3 (\ell+1)T^{-4}\leq T^{-3} .
If, instead, 𝖢𝗈𝗌𝗍 ⁡ ( x L ) ≤ γ ​ OPT \operatorname{{\sf Cost}}(x_{L})\leq\gamma\mathrm{OPT} , we incur zero γ \gamma -regret.
Together, 𝔼 ​ [ R > L ∣ ℒ ] ≤ C max ​ T ⋅ T − 3 \mathbb{E}[R_{>L}\mid\mathcal{L}]\leq C_{\max}T\cdot T^{-3} . Now suppose ℒ \mathcal{L} does not occur.
In this case, period T T was reached before finding an approximate local optimum, midway through executing an incomplete phase L + 1 L+1 .
We can use similar techniques to bound R > L R_{>L} for this incomplete phase as we for R ℓ R_{\ell} above, in fact, we can simply consider R > L R_{>L} to be R L + 1 R_{L+1} .
It follows from the same arguments as before that Putting everything together, Summing all the terms together, we have: where in the last step we used the bound on L L from Lemma ˜ 3.11 .
This establishes Theorem ˜ 3.1 .

sections.heading: 3.1 Analysis
sections.body:
We now perform the analysis which will yield Theorem ˜ 3.1 .
First, we encapsulate our applications of concentration inequalities into the following lemma which we will apply several times. Fix a solution x ∈ 𝒳 x\in\mathcal{X} and let z 1 , z 2 , … , z N z_{1},z_{2},\ldots,z_{N} be N := 3 ​ C max ​ ( 4 ​ log ⁡ T + log ⁡ M ) / ( δ 2 ​ α 2 ​ θ ) N:=3C_{\max}(4\log T+\log M)/(\delta^{2}\alpha^{2}\theta) independent samples from 𝒟 \mathcal{D} , where ℓ , M ∈ ℕ \ell,M\in\mathbb{N} , θ > 0 \theta>0 , α ∈ ( 0 , 1 ) \alpha\in(0,1) , and δ = 1 − α 1 + α ∈ ( 0 , 1 ) \delta=\frac{1-\alpha}{1+\alpha}\in(0,1) .
Let 𝖢𝗈𝗌𝗍 ^ ​ ( x ) = 1 N ​ ∑ s = 1 N 𝖢𝗈𝗌𝗍 ⁡ ( x , z s ) \widehat{\operatorname{{\sf Cost}}}(x)=\frac{1}{N}\sum_{s=1}^{N}\operatorname{{\sf Cost}}(x,z_{s}) . Then (a) 𝖢𝗈𝗌𝗍 ⁡ ( x ) ≤ α 2 ​ θ ⟹ Pr z 1 , z 2 , … , z N ∼ 𝒟 N ⁡ [ 𝖢𝗈𝗌𝗍 ^ ​ ( x ) > 2 ​ α 2 1 + α ​ θ ] ≤ M − 1 ​ T − 4 \operatorname{{\sf Cost}}(x)\leq\alpha^{2}\theta\implies\Pr_{z_{1},z_{2},\ldots,z_{N}\sim\mathcal{D}^{N}}\left[\widehat{\operatorname{{\sf Cost}}}(x)>\frac{2\alpha^{2}}{1+\alpha}\theta\right]\leq M^{-1}T^{-4} , and 𝖢𝗈𝗌𝗍 ⁡ ( x ) ≤ α 2 ​ θ ⟹ Pr z 1 , z 2 , … , z N ∼ 𝒟 N ⁡ [ 𝖢𝗈𝗌𝗍 ^ ​ ( x ) > 2 ​ α 2 1 + α ​ θ ] ≤ M − 1 ​ T − 4 \operatorname{{\sf Cost}}(x)\leq\alpha^{2}\theta\implies\Pr_{z_{1},z_{2},\ldots,z_{N}\sim\mathcal{D}^{N}}\left[\widehat{\operatorname{{\sf Cost}}}(x)>\frac{2\alpha^{2}}{1+\alpha}\theta\right]\leq M^{-1}T^{-4} , and (b) 𝖢𝗈𝗌𝗍 ⁡ ( x ) ≥ α ​ θ ⟹ Pr z 1 , z 2 , … , z N ∼ 𝒟 N ⁡ [ 𝖢𝗈𝗌𝗍 ^ ​ ( x ) ≤ 2 ​ α 2 1 + α ​ θ ] ≤ M − 1 ​ T − 4 \operatorname{{\sf Cost}}(x)\geq\alpha\theta\implies\Pr_{z_{1},z_{2},\ldots,z_{N}\sim\mathcal{D}^{N}}\left[\widehat{\operatorname{{\sf Cost}}}(x)\leq\frac{2\alpha^{2}}{1+\alpha}\theta\right]\leq M^{-1}T^{-4} . 𝖢𝗈𝗌𝗍 ⁡ ( x ) ≥ α ​ θ ⟹ Pr z 1 , z 2 , … , z N ∼ 𝒟 N ⁡ [ 𝖢𝗈𝗌𝗍 ^ ​ ( x ) ≤ 2 ​ α 2 1 + α ​ θ ] ≤ M − 1 ​ T − 4 \operatorname{{\sf Cost}}(x)\geq\alpha\theta\implies\Pr_{z_{1},z_{2},\ldots,z_{N}\sim\mathcal{D}^{N}}\left[\widehat{\operatorname{{\sf Cost}}}(x)\leq\frac{2\alpha^{2}}{1+\alpha}\theta\right]\leq M^{-1}T^{-4} . For brevity, we write Pr ⁡ [ ⋅ ] := Pr z 1 , z 2 , … , z N ∼ 𝒟 N ⁡ [ ⋅ ] \Pr[\cdot]:=\Pr_{z_{1},z_{2},\ldots,z_{N}\sim\mathcal{D}^{N}}[\cdot] .
Setting X s = 𝖢𝗈𝗌𝗍 ⁡ ( x , z s ) / C max X_{s}=\operatorname{{\sf Cost}}(x,z_{s})/C_{\max} for all s ∈ [ N ] s\in[N] , we have that X s ∈ [ 0 , 1 ] X_{s}\in[0,1] and 𝔼 ​ [ X s ] = 𝖢𝗈𝗌𝗍 ⁡ ( x ) / C max \mathbb{E}[X_{s}]=\operatorname{{\sf Cost}}(x)/C_{\max} .
Observe that 2 ​ α 2 1 + α = ( 1 + δ ) ​ α 2 = ( 1 − δ ) ​ α \frac{2\alpha^{2}}{1+\alpha}=(1+\delta)\alpha^{2}=(1-\delta)\alpha for our choice of δ \delta .
For part (a), when 𝖢𝗈𝗌𝗍 ⁡ ( x ) ≤ α 2 ​ θ \operatorname{{\sf Cost}}(x)\leq\alpha^{2}\theta , observe that Since X s ∈ [ 0 , 1 ] X_{s}\in[0,1] and 𝔼 ​ [ X s ] ≤ α 2 ​ θ / C max \mathbb{E}[X_{s}]\leq\alpha^{2}\theta/C_{\max} , we may bound the right hand side using Theorem ˜ 2.3 as where the right hand side follows from our choice of N N . For part (b), when 𝖢𝗈𝗌𝗍 ⁡ ( x ) ≥ α ​ θ \operatorname{{\sf Cost}}(x)\geq\alpha\theta , it follows that Again, since X s ∈ [ 0 , 1 ] X_{s}\in[0,1] and 𝔼 ​ [ X s ] ≥ α ​ θ / C max \mathbb{E}[X_{s}]\geq\alpha\theta/C_{\max} , we bound the right hand side using Theorem ˜ 2.3 as where the right hand side follows from our choice of N N and α ≤ 1 \alpha\leq 1 . This completes the proof.
∎ Let L L be the index of the (random) last phase that is encountered in Algorithm ˜ 2 .
To bound the overall regret, we will bound the total regret in phases with index ℓ < L \ell<L and also show that the regret in the last phase L L is negligible.
Before we can do that, we need to define an appropriate sequence of “bad events” which are exceedingly unlikely, for which will be able to bound the regret when conditioning on their negation, as is standard. For each phase ℓ \ell , let ℳ ℓ \mathcal{M}_{\ell} be the event that Algorithm ˜ 2 makes it to phase ℓ \ell and let 𝒞 ℓ \mathcal{C}_{\ell} be the event that 𝖢𝗈𝗌𝗍 ⁡ ( x ℓ ) > θ ℓ \operatorname{{\sf Cost}}(x_{\ell})>\theta_{\ell} .
Then we define the bad event in phase ℓ \ell as ℬ ℓ := ℳ ℓ ∧ 𝒞 ℓ \mathcal{B}_{\ell}:=\mathcal{M}_{\ell}\wedge\mathcal{C}_{\ell} and 𝒢 ℓ = ¬ ℬ ℓ \mathcal{G}_{\ell}=\neg\mathcal{B}_{\ell} . We will show that these events are unlikely.
Intuitively, this will follow inductively by assuming that ℬ ℓ \mathcal{B}_{\ell} is unlikely, and then we can use the definition of our algorithm to bound Pr ⁡ [ ℬ ℓ + 1 ∣ 𝒢 ℓ ] \Pr[\mathcal{B}_{\ell+1}\mid\mathcal{G}_{\ell}] and show that ℬ ℓ + 1 \mathcal{B}_{\ell+1} is unlikely via a standard decomposition.
The key step involves bounding Pr ⁡ [ ℬ ℓ + 1 ∣ 𝒢 ℓ ] \Pr[\mathcal{B}_{\ell+1}\mid\mathcal{G}_{\ell}] . For each ℓ \ell , we have Pr ⁡ [ ℬ ℓ + 1 ∣ 𝒢 ℓ ] ≤ T − 3 \Pr[\mathcal{B}_{\ell+1}\mid\mathcal{G}_{\ell}]\leq T^{-3} Proving this lemma requires careful analysis of the TestNeighborhood subroutine ( Algorithm ˜ 3 ), which we postpone momentarily.
For now, we show Lemma ˜ 3.4 implies a bound on Pr ⁡ [ ℬ ℓ ] \Pr[\mathcal{B}_{\ell}] . For each ℓ \ell , we have Pr ⁡ [ ℬ ℓ ] ≤ ℓ ​ T − 3 \Pr[\mathcal{B}_{\ell}]\leq\ell T^{-3} . We proceed by induction on ℓ \ell .
For the base case when ℓ = 1 \ell=1 , we have that 𝖢𝗈𝗌𝗍 ⁡ ( x 1 ) ≤ C max ≤ θ 1 \operatorname{{\sf Cost}}(x_{1})\leq C_{\max}\leq\theta_{1} , and so Pr ⁡ [ ℬ 1 ] = 0 ≤ T − 3 \Pr[\mathcal{B}_{1}]=0\leq T^{-3} , proving the base case. Inductively, we assume that Pr ⁡ [ ℬ ℓ ] ≤ ℓ ​ T − 3 \Pr[\mathcal{B}_{\ell}]\leq\ell T^{-3} and aim to bound Pr ⁡ [ ℬ ℓ + 1 ] \Pr[\mathcal{B}_{\ell+1}] .
By the law of total probability we have completing the proof of the inductive case and giving the corollary.
∎ We now turn to analyzing Algorithm ˜ 3 .
Consider a phase ℓ \ell of Algorithm ˜ 2 in which Algorithm ˜ 3 is called on solution x ℓ x_{\ell} and fix an iteration of the outer ‘for’ loop in Algorithm ˜ 3 in which solution x ′ ∈ 𝒩 ​ ( x ℓ ) x^{\prime}\in\mathcal{N}(x_{\ell}) is considered.
We call each iteration of the inner ‘for’ loop in Algorithm ˜ 3 a sub-phase , of which there are at most ℓ \ell for each neighboring solution x ′ ∈ 𝒩 ​ ( x ) x^{\prime}\in\mathcal{N}(x) .
Ideally, if 𝖢𝗈𝗌𝗍 ⁡ ( x ′ ) > α ​ θ ℓ \operatorname{{\sf Cost}}(x^{\prime})>\alpha\theta_{\ell} , then we do not make it past sub-phase ℓ \ell when considering solution x ′ x^{\prime} (and actually, we need to be slightly more careful than this).
The tools we develop here are needed to bound the regret incurred by using each neighboring solution x ′ x^{\prime} across each sub-phase.
To start, we introduce the following definitions. For each x ′ ∈ 𝒩 ​ ( x ) x^{\prime}\in\mathcal{N}(x) , we say that x ′ x^{\prime} is a bad neighbor of x x if 𝖢𝗈𝗌𝗍 ⁡ ( x ′ ) ≥ α ​ θ ℓ \operatorname{{\sf Cost}}(x^{\prime})\geq\alpha\theta_{\ell} .
Additionally, let 𝒩 B ​ ( x ) ⊆ 𝒩 ​ ( x ) \mathcal{N}_{B}(x)\subseteq\mathcal{N}(x) be the set of bad neighbors of x x and let ℓ ′ ​ ( x ′ ) ∈ [ ℓ ] \ell^{\prime}(x^{\prime})\in[\ell] be the sub-phase index such that 𝖢𝗈𝗌𝗍 ⁡ ( x ′ ) ∈ [ α ​ θ ℓ ′ ​ ( x ′ ) , θ ℓ ′ ​ ( x ′ ) ] \operatorname{{\sf Cost}}(x^{\prime})\in[\alpha\theta_{\ell^{\prime}(x^{\prime})},\theta_{\ell^{\prime}(x^{\prime})}] for each x ′ ∈ 𝒩 B ​ ( x ) x^{\prime}\in\mathcal{N}_{B}(x) . Let ℬ ℓ ′ \mathcal{B}^{\prime}_{\ell} be the event that in phase ℓ \ell some bad neighbor x ′ ∈ 𝒩 B ​ ( x ℓ ) x^{\prime}\in\mathcal{N}_{B}(x_{\ell}) makes it past sub-phase ℓ ′ ​ ( x ′ ) \ell^{\prime}(x^{\prime}) when Algorithm ˜ 3 is called. Note that if any bad neighbor x ′ x^{\prime} makes it past sub-phase ℓ \ell , then Algorithm ˜ 3 returns x ′ x^{\prime} .
Further, if any bad neighbor x ′ x^{\prime} makes it past sub-phase ℓ ′ ​ ( x ′ ) \ell^{\prime}(x^{\prime}) , then we may incur too much regret from testing x ′ x^{\prime} in later sub-phases since 𝖢𝗈𝗌𝗍 ⁡ ( x ′ ) ≥ α ​ θ ℓ ′ ​ ( x ′ ) \operatorname{{\sf Cost}}(x^{\prime})\geq\alpha\theta_{\ell^{\prime}(x^{\prime})} .
We show that ℬ ℓ ′ \mathcal{B}^{\prime}_{\ell} is unlikely, and conclude that bad neighbors are highly likely to be removed from contention early enough to avoid incurring high regret. Pr ⁡ [ ℬ ℓ ′ ] ≤ T − 4 \Pr[\mathcal{B}^{\prime}_{\ell}]\leq T^{-4} . In the case that Algorithm ˜ 3 is not called in phase ℓ \ell , then the probability is at most 0 since ℬ ℓ ′ \mathcal{B}^{\prime}_{\ell} cannot happen.
Formalizing this, let 𝒯 ℓ \mathcal{T}_{\ell} be the event that Algorithm ˜ 3 is called in phase ℓ \ell , then we have Pr ⁡ [ ℬ ℓ ′ ∣ ¬ 𝒯 ℓ ] = 0 \Pr[\mathcal{B}^{\prime}_{\ell}\mid\neg\mathcal{T}_{\ell}]=0 .
In the other case where Algorithm ˜ 3 is called in phase ℓ \ell , then we need to show that it is unlikely for any bad neighbor x ′ x^{\prime} to make it past sub-phase ℓ ′ ​ ( x ′ ) \ell^{\prime}(x^{\prime}) .
To this end, fix a bad neighbor x ′ x^{\prime} .
If x ′ x^{\prime} doesn’t make it to sub-phase ℓ ′ ​ ( x ′ ) ≤ ℓ \ell^{\prime}(x^{\prime})\leq\ell , then it clearly doesn’t make it past sub-phase ℓ \ell .
Thus in order for event ℬ ℓ ′ \mathcal{B}^{\prime}_{\ell} to happen due to x ′ x^{\prime} , we must have that x ′ x^{\prime} makes it to sub-phase ℓ ′ ​ ( x ′ ) \ell^{\prime}(x^{\prime}) and makes it past this sub-phase.
Now, x ′ x^{\prime} only makes it past sub-phase ℓ ′ ​ ( x ′ ) \ell^{\prime}(x^{\prime}) when 𝖢𝗈𝗌𝗍 ^ ​ ( x ′ ) ≤ ( 2 ​ α 2 / ( 1 + α ) ) ​ θ ℓ ′ ​ ( x ′ ) \widehat{\operatorname{{\sf Cost}}}(x^{\prime})\leq(2\alpha^{2}/(1+\alpha))\theta_{\ell^{\prime}(x^{\prime})} , where 𝖢𝗈𝗌𝗍 ^ ​ ( x ′ ) \widehat{\operatorname{{\sf Cost}}}(x^{\prime}) is the average of N ℓ ′ ​ ( x ′ ) N_{\ell^{\prime}(x^{\prime})} independent samples with distribution 𝖢𝗈𝗌𝗍 ⁡ ( x ′ , Z ) \operatorname{{\sf Cost}}(x^{\prime},Z) , where Z ∼ 𝒟 Z\sim\mathcal{D} .
By part(b) of Lemma ˜ 3.2 , the probability of this is at most M − 1 ​ T − 4 M^{-1}T^{-4} . It follows that Finally, observe that which completes the proof.
∎ Lemma ˜ 3.8 establishes that Algorithm ˜ 3 filters out bad neighboring solutions with high probability.
In order to prove Lemma ˜ 3.4 , we also need to show that good neighboring solutions are unlikely to be filtered out.
More specifically, there are three situations to consider (all under the condition that 𝖢𝗈𝗌𝗍 ⁡ ( x ℓ ) ≤ θ ℓ \operatorname{{\sf Cost}}(x_{\ell})\leq\theta_{\ell} ): (1) every solution in 𝒩 ​ ( x ℓ ) \mathcal{N}(x_{\ell}) is a bad neighbor, (2) some solution x ′ ∈ 𝒩 ​ ( x ℓ ) x^{\prime}\in\mathcal{N}(x_{\ell}) satisfies 𝖢𝗈𝗌𝗍 ⁡ ( x ′ ) ≤ α 2 ​ θ ℓ \operatorname{{\sf Cost}}(x^{\prime})\leq\alpha^{2}\theta_{\ell} , and (3) not all neighboring solutions are bad, but all solutions that are not bad have 𝖢𝗈𝗌𝗍 ⁡ ( x ′ ) ∈ [ α 2 ​ θ ℓ , α ​ θ ℓ ] \operatorname{{\sf Cost}}(x^{\prime})\in[\alpha^{2}\theta_{\ell},\alpha\theta_{\ell}] . In situation (1), it is a simple corollary of Lemma ˜ 3.8 that Algorithm ˜ 3 will output x ℓ x_{\ell} with high probability, indicating that we did not find an improved solution (and therefore 𝖢𝗈𝗌𝗍 ⁡ ( x ℓ ) ≤ γ ​ OPT \operatorname{{\sf Cost}}(x_{\ell})\leq\gamma\mathrm{OPT} ).
In situation (2), where there is a neighboring solution x ′ x^{\prime} with 𝖢𝗈𝗌𝗍 ⁡ ( x ′ ) ≤ α 2 ​ θ ℓ \operatorname{{\sf Cost}}(x^{\prime})\leq\alpha^{2}\theta_{\ell} , it is possible that Algorithm ˜ 3 considers and returns a different neighboring solution x ′′ x^{\prime\prime} with 𝖢𝗈𝗌𝗍 ⁡ ( x ′′ ) ∈ [ α 2 ​ θ ℓ , α ​ θ ℓ ] \operatorname{{\sf Cost}}(x^{\prime\prime})\in[\alpha^{2}\theta_{\ell},\alpha\theta_{\ell}] (again it is unlikely to return a bad neighbor due to Lemma ˜ 3.8 ). However, if x ′ x^{\prime} is ever considered it will survive past sub-phase ℓ \ell with high probability ( Lemmas ˜ 3.2 and 3.8 ) and thus be returned by Algorithm ˜ 3 .
Either outcome is acceptable and guarantees at least an α \alpha -factor improvement.
In situation (3), Algorithm ˜ 3 may output x ℓ x_{\ell} , indicating no improved solution.
This follows since, under the event that 𝖢𝗈𝗌𝗍 ⁡ ( x ℓ ) ≤ θ ℓ \operatorname{{\sf Cost}}(x_{\ell})\leq\theta_{\ell} , each neighboring solution has 𝖢𝗈𝗌𝗍 ⁡ ( x ′ ) ≥ α ​ θ ℓ ≥ β ​ 𝖢𝗈𝗌𝗍 ⁡ ( x ℓ ) \operatorname{{\sf Cost}}(x^{\prime})\geq\alpha\theta_{\ell}\geq\beta\operatorname{{\sf Cost}}(x_{\ell}) , which by the ( β , γ ) (\beta,\gamma) -improving moves condition implies that 𝖢𝗈𝗌𝗍 ⁡ ( x ℓ ) ≤ γ ​ OPT \operatorname{{\sf Cost}}(x_{\ell})\leq\gamma\mathrm{OPT} .
It is also acceptable for Algorithm ˜ 3 to output a solution with cost in the interval [ α 2 ​ θ ℓ , α ​ θ ℓ ] [\alpha^{2}\theta_{\ell},\alpha\theta_{\ell}] , since that guarantees an α \alpha -factor improvement.
The following lemma formalizes this. Suppose that Algorithm ˜ 3 is run in phase ℓ \ell with solution x ℓ x_{\ell} and let x 𝖭𝖾𝗐 x_{\operatorname{{\sf New}}} be the random solution which it outputs.
Define the events ℱ ℓ , 1 , ℱ ℓ , 2 , ℱ ℓ , 3 \mathcal{F}_{\ell,1},\mathcal{F}_{\ell,2},\mathcal{F}_{\ell,3} as follows: • ℱ ℓ , 1 = { 𝒩 B ​ ( x ℓ ) = 𝒩 ​ ( x ℓ ) } \mathcal{F}_{\ell,1}=\{\mathcal{N}_{B}(x_{\ell})=\mathcal{N}(x_{\ell})\} (all neighbors of x ℓ x_{\ell} are bad) ℱ ℓ , 1 = { 𝒩 B ​ ( x ℓ ) = 𝒩 ​ ( x ℓ ) } \mathcal{F}_{\ell,1}=\{\mathcal{N}_{B}(x_{\ell})=\mathcal{N}(x_{\ell})\} (all neighbors of x ℓ x_{\ell} are bad) • ℱ ℓ , 2 = { ∃ x ′ ∈ 𝒩 ​ ( x ℓ ) , 𝖢𝗈𝗌𝗍 ⁡ ( x ′ ) ≤ α 2 ​ θ ℓ } \mathcal{F}_{\ell,2}=\{\exists x^{\prime}\in\mathcal{N}(x_{\ell}),\operatorname{{\sf Cost}}(x^{\prime})\leq\alpha^{2}\theta_{\ell}\} (there is a neighbor with a β = α 2 \beta=\alpha^{2} -factor decrease) ℱ ℓ , 2 = { ∃ x ′ ∈ 𝒩 ​ ( x ℓ ) , 𝖢𝗈𝗌𝗍 ⁡ ( x ′ ) ≤ α 2 ​ θ ℓ } \mathcal{F}_{\ell,2}=\{\exists x^{\prime}\in\mathcal{N}(x_{\ell}),\operatorname{{\sf Cost}}(x^{\prime})\leq\alpha^{2}\theta_{\ell}\} (there is a neighbor with a β = α 2 \beta=\alpha^{2} -factor decrease) • ℱ ℓ , 3 = { ∀ x ′ ∈ 𝒩 ( x ℓ ) ∖ 𝒩 B ( x ℓ ) ≠ ∅ , 𝖢𝗈𝗌𝗍 ( x ′ ) ∈ [ α 2 θ ℓ , α θ ℓ ] } \mathcal{F}_{\ell,3}=\{\forall x^{\prime}\in\mathcal{N}(x_{\ell})\setminus\mathcal{N}_{B}(x_{\ell})\neq\emptyset,\operatorname{{\sf Cost}}(x^{\prime})\in[\alpha^{2}\theta_{\ell},\alpha\theta_{\ell}]\} (no neighbor has an β = α 2 \beta=\alpha^{2} -factor decrease, but there is one with an α \alpha -factor decrease). ℱ ℓ , 3 = { ∀ x ′ ∈ 𝒩 ( x ℓ ) ∖ 𝒩 B ( x ℓ ) ≠ ∅ , 𝖢𝗈𝗌𝗍 ( x ′ ) ∈ [ α 2 θ ℓ , α θ ℓ ] } \mathcal{F}_{\ell,3}=\{\forall x^{\prime}\in\mathcal{N}(x_{\ell})\setminus\mathcal{N}_{B}(x_{\ell})\neq\emptyset,\operatorname{{\sf Cost}}(x^{\prime})\in[\alpha^{2}\theta_{\ell},\alpha\theta_{\ell}]\} (no neighbor has an β = α 2 \beta=\alpha^{2} -factor decrease, but there is one with an α \alpha -factor decrease). Then (a) Pr ⁡ [ x 𝖭𝖾𝗐 ≠ x ℓ ∣ ∧ ℱ ℓ , 1 ] ≤ T − 4 \Pr[x_{\operatorname{{\sf New}}}\neq x_{\ell}\mid\wedge\mathcal{F}_{\ell,1}]\leq T^{-4} Pr ⁡ [ x 𝖭𝖾𝗐 ≠ x ℓ ∣ ∧ ℱ ℓ , 1 ] ≤ T − 4 \Pr[x_{\operatorname{{\sf New}}}\neq x_{\ell}\mid\wedge\mathcal{F}_{\ell,1}]\leq T^{-4} (b) Pr ⁡ [ 𝖢𝗈𝗌𝗍 ⁡ ( x 𝖭𝖾𝗐 ) > α ​ θ ℓ ∣ ℱ ℓ , 2 ] ≤ ( ℓ + 1 ) ​ T − 4 \Pr[\operatorname{{\sf Cost}}(x_{\operatorname{{\sf New}}})>\alpha\theta_{\ell}\mid\mathcal{F}_{\ell,2}]\leq(\ell+1)T^{-4} Pr ⁡ [ 𝖢𝗈𝗌𝗍 ⁡ ( x 𝖭𝖾𝗐 ) > α ​ θ ℓ ∣ ℱ ℓ , 2 ] ≤ ( ℓ + 1 ) ​ T − 4 \Pr[\operatorname{{\sf Cost}}(x_{\operatorname{{\sf New}}})>\alpha\theta_{\ell}\mid\mathcal{F}_{\ell,2}]\leq(\ell+1)T^{-4} (c) Pr ⁡ [ ( 𝖢𝗈𝗌𝗍 ⁡ ( x 𝖭𝖾𝗐 ) > α ​ θ ℓ ) ∧ ( x 𝖭𝖾𝗐 ≠ x ℓ ) ∣ ℱ ℓ , 3 ] ≤ T − 4 \Pr[(\operatorname{{\sf Cost}}(x_{\operatorname{{\sf New}}})>\alpha\theta_{\ell})\wedge(x_{\operatorname{{\sf New}}}\neq x_{\ell})\mid\mathcal{F}_{\ell,3}]\leq T^{-4} Pr ⁡ [ ( 𝖢𝗈𝗌𝗍 ⁡ ( x 𝖭𝖾𝗐 ) > α ​ θ ℓ ) ∧ ( x 𝖭𝖾𝗐 ≠ x ℓ ) ∣ ℱ ℓ , 3 ] ≤ T − 4 \Pr[(\operatorname{{\sf Cost}}(x_{\operatorname{{\sf New}}})>\alpha\theta_{\ell})\wedge(x_{\operatorname{{\sf New}}}\neq x_{\ell})\mid\mathcal{F}_{\ell,3}]\leq T^{-4} Part (a):
Conditioned on ℱ ℓ , 1 \mathcal{F}_{\ell,1} , the only way that x 𝖭𝖾𝗐 ≠ x ℓ x_{\operatorname{{\sf New}}}\neq x_{\ell} is if a bad neighboring solution makes it past sub-phase ℓ \ell . This implies that event ℬ ℓ ′ \mathcal{B}^{\prime}_{\ell} occurs, and it follows by Lemma ˜ 3.8 that Part (b): There are two ways we can have 𝖢𝗈𝗌𝗍 ⁡ ( x 𝖭𝖾𝗐 ) > α ​ θ ℓ \operatorname{{\sf Cost}}(x_{\operatorname{{\sf New}}})>\alpha\theta_{\ell} under condition ℱ ℓ , 2 \mathcal{F}_{\ell,2} .
One way is that a bad neighbor has been output as x 𝖭𝖾𝗐 x_{\operatorname{{\sf New}}} , and thus ℬ ℓ ′ \mathcal{B}^{\prime}_{\ell} has occurred as discussed previously.
The other way is if x 𝖭𝖾𝗐 = x ℓ x_{\operatorname{{\sf New}}}=x_{\ell} and 𝖢𝗈𝗌𝗍 ⁡ ( x ℓ ) > α ​ θ ℓ \operatorname{{\sf Cost}}(x_{\ell})>\alpha\theta_{\ell} , in which case it must be that the solution x ′ x^{\prime} with 𝖢𝗈𝗌𝗍 ⁡ ( x ℓ ) ≤ α 2 ​ θ ℓ \operatorname{{\sf Cost}}(x_{\ell})\leq\alpha^{2}\theta_{\ell} was considered but did not make it past sub-phase ℓ \ell .
Let ℬ ℓ ′ ​ ( x ′ ) \mathcal{B}^{\prime}_{\ell}(x^{\prime}) denote this latter event.
Thus by a union bound we have Again, from Lemma ˜ 3.8 , Pr ⁡ [ ℬ ℓ ′ ] ≤ T − 4 \Pr[\mathcal{B}^{\prime}_{\ell}]\leq T^{-4} . It remains to bound Pr ⁡ [ ℬ ℓ ′ ​ ( x ′ ) ] \Pr[\mathcal{B}^{\prime}_{\ell}(x^{\prime})] .
This event happens if x ′ x^{\prime} fails at least one of the checks that occur when x ′ x^{\prime} is considered in the inner ‘for’ loop of Algorithm ˜ 3 .
By part (a) of Lemma ˜ 3.2 and a union bound, we have Combining these bounds completes part (b). Part (c): We can only have 𝖢𝗈𝗌𝗍 ⁡ ( x ℓ ) > α ​ θ ℓ \operatorname{{\sf Cost}}(x_{\ell})>\alpha\theta_{\ell} and x 𝖭𝖾𝗐 ≠ x ℓ x_{\operatorname{{\sf New}}}\neq x_{\ell} if some bad neighbor makes it past sub-phase ℓ \ell , which can only occur if ℬ ℓ ′ \mathcal{B}^{\prime}_{\ell} has occurred.
Thus we have by Lemma ˜ 3.8 .
This yields the claim for the last case and completes the proof of the lemma.
∎ As a direct corollary of Lemma ˜ 3.9 , we get that Algorithm ˜ 3 is unlikely to output a solution with cost more than α ​ θ ℓ \alpha\theta_{\ell} when run in phase ℓ \ell . Suppose that Algorithm ˜ 3 is run in phase ℓ \ell with solution x ℓ x_{\ell} and let x 𝖭𝖾𝗐 x_{\operatorname{{\sf New}}} be the random solution which it outputs. Then we have that Pr ⁡ [ 𝖢𝗈𝗌𝗍 ⁡ ( x 𝖭𝖾𝗐 ) > α ​ θ ℓ ] ≤ ( ℓ + 1 ) ​ T − 4 \Pr[\operatorname{{\sf Cost}}(x_{\operatorname{{\sf New}}})>\alpha\theta_{\ell}]\leq(\ell+1)T^{-4} . Under event ℱ ℓ , 1 \mathcal{F}_{\ell,1} , the only way for Algorithm ˜ 3 to output a bad solution is to output something other than x ℓ x_{\ell} .
Similarly, the only for Algorithm ˜ 3 to output a bad solution under event ℱ ℓ , 3 \mathcal{F}_{\ell,3} is to output something other than x ℓ x_{\ell} which happens to be bad.
Then it follows from the law of total probability and Lemma ˜ 3.9 that ∎ Now we return to the proof of Lemma ˜ 3.4 which will utilize the tools we developed from the analysis of Algorithm ˜ 3 . Recall that 𝒢 ℓ = ¬ 𝒞 ℓ ∨ ¬ ℳ ℓ \mathcal{G}_{\ell}=\neg\mathcal{C}_{\ell}\vee\neg\mathcal{M}_{\ell} , i.e., the good event for phase ℓ \ell happens if either Algorithm ˜ 2 doesn’t make it to phase ℓ \ell or we have 𝖢𝗈𝗌𝗍 ⁡ ( x ℓ ) ≤ θ ℓ \operatorname{{\sf Cost}}(x_{\ell})\leq\theta_{\ell} .
First, we claim that Pr ⁡ [ ℬ ℓ + 1 ∣ 𝒢 ℓ ] ≤ Pr ⁡ [ ℬ ℓ + 1 ∣ ¬ 𝒞 ℓ ] \Pr[\mathcal{B}_{\ell+1}\mid\mathcal{G}_{\ell}]\leq\Pr[\mathcal{B}_{\ell+1}\mid\neg\mathcal{C}_{\ell}] .
To see this, we have: The first line follows from the definition of 𝒢 ℓ \mathcal{G}_{\ell} and conditional probability while the second line follows from the distributive law for ∧ \wedge and ∨ \vee .
We use a union bound in the third line and the observation that 𝒢 ℓ \mathcal{G}_{\ell} contains both ¬ 𝒞 ℓ \neg\mathcal{C}_{\ell} and ¬ ℳ ℓ \neg\mathcal{M}_{\ell} in the fourth line.
To finish, the fifth line is again the definition of conditional probability and the last step uses the observation that Pr ⁡ [ ℬ ℓ + 1 ∣ ¬ ℳ ℓ ] = 0 \Pr[\mathcal{B}_{\ell+1}\mid\neg\mathcal{M}_{\ell}]=0 since it is impossible for the algorithm to make it to phase ℓ + 1 \ell+1 if it has not made it to phase ℓ \ell . Thus we may focus on bounding Pr ⁡ [ ℬ ℓ + 1 ∣ ¬ 𝒞 ℓ ] \Pr[\mathcal{B}_{\ell+1}\mid\neg\mathcal{C}_{\ell}] .
Under condition ¬ 𝒞 ℓ \neg\mathcal{C}_{\ell} , 𝖢𝗈𝗌𝗍 ⁡ ( x ℓ ) ≤ θ ℓ \operatorname{{\sf Cost}}(x_{\ell})\leq\theta_{\ell} by definition.
We analyze three cases depending on how 𝖢𝗈𝗌𝗍 ⁡ ( x ℓ ) \operatorname{{\sf Cost}}(x_{\ell}) relates to θ ℓ \theta_{\ell} . In this case, we have 𝖢𝗈𝗌𝗍 ⁡ ( x ℓ ) ≤ α ​ θ ℓ + 1 \operatorname{{\sf Cost}}(x_{\ell})\leq\alpha\theta_{\ell+1} and the current cost is small enough to move to phase ℓ + 1 \ell+1 .
Denote the event that 𝖢𝗈𝗌𝗍 ⁡ ( x ℓ ) ≤ α 2 ​ θ ℓ \operatorname{{\sf Cost}}(x_{\ell})\leq\alpha^{2}\theta_{\ell} by ℰ ℓ , 1 \mathcal{E}_{\ell,1} .
We will show that Pr ⁡ [ ℬ ℓ + 1 ∣ ¬ 𝒞 ℓ ∧ ℰ ℓ , 1 ] \Pr[\mathcal{B}_{\ell+1}\mid\neg\mathcal{C}_{\ell}\wedge\mathcal{E}_{\ell,1}] is small.
If Algorithm ˜ 2 moves to phase ℓ + 1 \ell+1 with x ℓ + 1 = x ℓ x_{\ell+1}=x_{\ell} , then 𝖢𝗈𝗌𝗍 ⁡ ( x ℓ + 1 ) < θ ℓ + 1 \operatorname{{\sf Cost}}(x_{\ell+1})<\theta_{\ell+1} so ℬ ℓ + 1 \mathcal{B}_{\ell+1} doesn’t happen.
Thus the only way for ℬ ℓ + 1 \mathcal{B}_{\ell+1} to happen is if the “if” statement on line 10 evaluates to true, which occurs when 𝖢𝗈𝗌𝗍 ^ ​ ( x ℓ ) > 2 ​ α 2 ​ θ ℓ / ( 1 + α ) \widehat{\operatorname{{\sf Cost}}}(x_{\ell})>2\alpha^{2}\theta_{\ell}/(1+\alpha) .
We will show that this happens with small probability.
Since 𝖢𝗈𝗌𝗍 ^ ​ ( x ℓ ) \widehat{\operatorname{{\sf Cost}}}(x_{\ell}) is the average of N ℓ N_{\ell} independent samples each with mean 𝖢𝗈𝗌𝗍 ⁡ ( x ℓ ) \operatorname{{\sf Cost}}(x_{\ell}) , by the first part of Lemma ˜ 3.2 and the discussion above, we have completing the argument for this case. For this case, we want Algorithm ˜ 3 to be called with high probability and that it succeeds with high probability.
Denote the event that α ​ θ ℓ ≤ 𝖢𝗈𝗌𝗍 ⁡ ( x ℓ ) ≤ θ ℓ \alpha\theta_{\ell}\leq\operatorname{{\sf Cost}}(x_{\ell})\leq\theta_{\ell} by ℰ ℓ , 2 \mathcal{E}_{\ell,2} .
In this case, there are two ways that we could fail to satisfy 𝖢𝗈𝗌𝗍 ⁡ ( x ℓ + 1 ) ≤ θ ℓ + 1 \operatorname{{\sf Cost}}(x_{\ell+1})\leq\theta_{\ell+1} .
The first is by skipping the step which explores the neighborhood and moving to the next phase with x ℓ + 1 = x ℓ x_{\ell+1}=x_{\ell} .
This occurs when the “if” statement in line 10 of Algorithm ˜ 2 evaluates to 𝖥𝖺𝗅𝗌𝖾 \operatorname{{\sf False}} , which happens with probability at most M − 1 ​ T − 4 M^{-1}T^{-4} by part (b) of Lemma ˜ 3.2 .
Assuming Algorithm ˜ 3 is called, the second way that we could fail to satisfy 𝖢𝗈𝗌𝗍 ⁡ ( x ℓ + 1 ) ≤ θ ℓ + 1 \operatorname{{\sf Cost}}(x_{\ell+1})\leq\theta_{\ell+1} is if Algorithm ˜ 3 returns a bad neighboring solution. Corollary ˜ 3.10 shows that this occurs with probability at most ( ℓ + 1 ) ​ T − 4 ≤ T − 3 (\ell+1)T^{-4}\leq T^{-3} .
Combining the bounds completes the analysis of this case. This last case is more flexible; we use ℰ ℓ , 3 \mathcal{E}_{\ell,3} to denote the event that 𝖢𝗈𝗌𝗍 ⁡ ( x ℓ ) ∈ ( α 2 ​ θ ℓ , α ​ θ ℓ ) \operatorname{{\sf Cost}}(x_{\ell})\in(\alpha^{2}\theta_{\ell},\alpha\theta_{\ell}) .
Since 𝖢𝗈𝗌𝗍 ⁡ ( x ℓ ) ≤ α ​ θ ℓ \operatorname{{\sf Cost}}(x_{\ell})\leq\alpha\theta_{\ell} , we will be safe in either the situation that 𝖢𝗈𝗌𝗍 ^ ​ ( x ℓ ) > 2 ​ α 2 ​ θ ℓ / ( 1 + α ) \widehat{\operatorname{{\sf Cost}}}(x_{\ell})>2\alpha^{2}\theta_{\ell}/(1+\alpha) (and so we set x ℓ + 1 = x ℓ x_{\ell+1}=x_{\ell} ) or not (and so we use Algorithm ˜ 3 to determine x ℓ + 1 x_{\ell+1} ).
In the first situation, we clearly have 𝖢𝗈𝗌𝗍 ⁡ ( x ℓ ) ≤ α ​ θ ℓ = θ ℓ + 1 \operatorname{{\sf Cost}}(x_{\ell})\leq\alpha\theta_{\ell}=\theta_{\ell+1} .
In the other situation, we use the same argument as in the previous case to argue that the probability of setting x ℓ + 1 x_{\ell+1} to a bad neighbor is small.
Thus we conclude that Pr ⁡ [ ℬ ℓ + 1 ∣ ¬ 𝒞 ℓ ∧ ℰ ℓ , 3 ] ≤ T − 4 \Pr[\mathcal{B}_{\ell+1}\mid\neg\mathcal{C}_{\ell}\wedge\mathcal{E}_{\ell,3}]\leq T^{-4} Since the three cases above are exhaustive, i.e., ℰ ℓ , 1 ∨ ℰ ℓ , 2 ∨ ℰ ℓ , 1 ≡ 𝖳𝗋𝗎𝖾 \mathcal{E}_{\ell,1}\vee\mathcal{E}_{\ell,2}\vee\mathcal{E}_{\ell,1}\equiv\operatorname{{\sf True}} when conditioned on 𝒞 ℓ \mathcal{C}_{\ell} , we conclude by the law of total probability that completing the proof of this lemma.
∎ Let L L be the index of the last complete phase, i.e., Algorithm ˜ 2 either finds a local solution x 𝖫𝖺𝗌𝗍 x_{\operatorname{{\sf Last}}} in phase L L (which is then played until period T T ) or period T T is reached in phase L + 1 L+1 .
We will show that L L is logarithmic in T T , which will be helpful to establish our regret bound later. L ≤ log ⁡ ( T ) / log ⁡ ( 1 α ) L\leq\log(T)/\log(\frac{1}{\alpha}) By definition of L L we know that ∑ ℓ = 1 L N ℓ ≤ T \sum_{\ell=1}^{L}N_{\ell}\leq T , since this is just counting the number of plays due to line 7 in Algorithm ˜ 2 .
From the definition of N ℓ N_{\ell} , we have: Rearranging this inequality to solve for L L and simplifying yields result.
∎ To complete the analysis, we separately analyze the γ \gamma -regret incurred by the algorithm during phases 1 to L L and the γ \gamma -regret after phase L L . Consider phase ℓ \ell .
We incur regret from playing the solution x ℓ x_{\ell} and also from playing neighboring solutions when Algorithm ˜ 3 is called.
Let R ℓ , 1 R_{\ell,1} and R ℓ , 2 R_{\ell,2} represent each of these quantities, respectively.
We start by bounding R ℓ , 1 R_{\ell,1} .
Since OPT ≥ 0 \mathrm{OPT}\geq 0 , by the law of total expectation we have: The first inequality follows since 𝖢𝗈𝗌𝗍 ⁡ ( x ℓ ) ≤ θ ℓ \operatorname{{\sf Cost}}(x_{\ell})\leq\theta_{\ell} under ¬ ℬ ℓ \neg\mathcal{B}_{\ell} (giving the first term) and Corollary ˜ 3.5 (which bounds Pr [ ℬ ℓ ] ) \Pr[\mathcal{B}_{\ell}]) .
The second inequality follows from the definition of N ℓ N_{\ell} and the fact that N ℓ N_{\ell} and ℓ \ell are both smaller than T T . Bounding R ℓ , 2 R_{\ell,2} is similar, but we have to account for each sub-phase.
We see that: The first term in the first inequality follows since in the worst case we play each neighboring solution in all sub-phases, but under event ¬ ℬ ℓ ′ \neg\mathcal{B}^{\prime}_{\ell} we never play a solution with cost more than θ ℓ ′ \theta_{\ell^{\prime}} in each sub-phase ℓ ′ \ell^{\prime} .
The second term follows since, under ℬ ℓ ′ \mathcal{B}^{\prime}_{\ell} , we can’t play a solution of cost more than C max C_{\max} for more than T T steps and Pr ⁡ [ ℬ ℓ ′ ] ≤ T − 4 \Pr[\mathcal{B}^{\prime}_{\ell}]\leq T^{-4} by Lemma ˜ 3.8 . Let R > L R_{>L} denote the regret incurred after phase L L .
We need to consider both the case where Algorithm ˜ 2 terminated with a solution x 𝖫𝖺𝗌𝗍 x_{\operatorname{{\sf Last}}} and when it did not.
Denote the former event by ℒ \mathcal{L} .
Then we have We now focus on each conditional expectation and show that the regret is small in each. Consider the first case where we condition on ℒ \mathcal{L} , and so we have that Algorithm ˜ 2 terminated with some solution x 𝖫𝖺𝗌𝗍 x_{\operatorname{{\sf Last}}} which is used for all remaining rounds.
This happens when Algorithm ˜ 3 is run in phase L L and returns the solution x L x_{L} .
We want to show that it is unlikely for x L x_{L} to satisfy 𝖢𝗈𝗌𝗍 ⁡ ( x L ) > γ ​ OPT \operatorname{{\sf Cost}}(x_{L})>\gamma\mathrm{OPT} .
Suppose 𝖢𝗈𝗌𝗍 ⁡ ( x L ) > γ ​ OPT \operatorname{{\sf Cost}}(x_{L})>\gamma\mathrm{OPT} , then by Definition ˜ 2.2 , we have that 𝒩 ​ ( x ℓ ) \mathcal{N}(x_{\ell}) contains a solution x ′ x^{\prime} with 𝖢𝗈𝗌𝗍 ⁡ ( x ′ ) ≤ β ​ 𝖢𝗈𝗌𝗍 ⁡ ( x L ) = α 2 ​ 𝖢𝗈𝗌𝗍 ⁡ ( x L ) \operatorname{{\sf Cost}}(x^{\prime})\leq\beta\operatorname{{\sf Cost}}(x_{L})=\alpha^{2}\operatorname{{\sf Cost}}(x_{L}) .
This means that event ℱ ℓ , 2 \mathcal{F}_{\ell,2} has occurred, and by Lemma ˜ 3.9 the probability of Algorithm ˜ 3 yielding x L x_{L} as output is at most ( ℓ + 1 ) ​ T − 4 ≤ T − 3 (\ell+1)T^{-4}\leq T^{-3} .
If, instead, 𝖢𝗈𝗌𝗍 ⁡ ( x L ) ≤ γ ​ OPT \operatorname{{\sf Cost}}(x_{L})\leq\gamma\mathrm{OPT} , we incur zero γ \gamma -regret.
Together, 𝔼 ​ [ R > L ∣ ℒ ] ≤ C max ​ T ⋅ T − 3 \mathbb{E}[R_{>L}\mid\mathcal{L}]\leq C_{\max}T\cdot T^{-3} . Now suppose ℒ \mathcal{L} does not occur.
In this case, period T T was reached before finding an approximate local optimum, midway through executing an incomplete phase L + 1 L+1 .
We can use similar techniques to bound R > L R_{>L} for this incomplete phase as we for R ℓ R_{\ell} above, in fact, we can simply consider R > L R_{>L} to be R L + 1 R_{L+1} .
It follows from the same arguments as before that Putting everything together, Summing all the terms together, we have: where in the last step we used the bound on L L from Lemma ˜ 3.11 .
This establishes Theorem ˜ 3.1 .

sections.heading: 4 Applications of Our Framework
sections.body:
We consider the problem of scheduling stochastic jobs to minimize total completion time on a single machine.
Let 𝒟 \mathcal{D} be a distribution over job sizes which is unknown to the scheduler.
There are n n jobs with stochastic sizes P = ( P 1 , P 2 , … , P n ) ∼ 𝒟 P=(P_{1},P_{2},\ldots,P_{n})\sim\mathcal{D} .
Let μ j := 𝔼 ​ [ P j ] \mu_{j}:=\mathbb{E}[P_{j}] denote the expected size of job j j . We only require independence across time periods; a pair of jobs j j and j ′ j^{\prime} may have correlated size distributions.
We also make a standard assumption that the job size distribution is bounded and normalized to be in [ 0 , 1 ] [0,1] . A schedule is given by a permutation π : [ n ] → [ n ] \pi:[n]\to[n] specifying that job j j is scheduled in position π ​ ( j ) \pi(j) . Let Π n \Pi_{n} be the permutations on [ n ] [n] , the feasible schedules.
Given a schedule π \pi , let C j ​ ( π , P ) := P j + ∑ j ′ : π ​ ( j ′ ) < π ​ ( j ) P j ′ C_{j}(\pi,P):=P_{j}+\sum_{j^{\prime}:\pi(j^{\prime})<\pi(j)}P_{j^{\prime}} be the completion time of job j j under schedule π \pi and sizes P P .
After reordering the summation, the cost of a schedule π \pi is Let OPT := min π ⁡ 𝔼 P ∼ 𝒟 ​ [ 𝖢𝗈𝗌𝗍 ⁡ ( π , P ) ] \mathrm{OPT}:=\min_{\pi}\mathbb{E}_{P\sim\mathcal{D}}[\operatorname{{\sf Cost}}(\pi,P)] .
We focus on learning from limited feedback using fixed, non-preemptive schedules.
In the single machine case full-information case, it is well known that the optimal schedule is given by applying Smith’s rule to the expected job sizes: π ∗ \pi^{*} orders the jobs in non-decreasing order of μ j \mu_{j} , or shortest expected processing time first [ 44 , 46 ] . When learning to schedule with bandit feedback,
the scheduler gains information by interacting with unknown 𝒟 \mathcal{D} through the following process which occurs in each of T T discrete time periods.
In period t ∈ [ T ] t\in[T] , the scheduler commits to a schedule π t \pi^{t} before job sizes are realized according to an independent sample P t ∼ 𝒟 P^{t}\sim\mathcal{D} . Then the scheduler observes 𝖢𝗈𝗌𝗍 ⁡ ( π t , P t ) \operatorname{{\sf Cost}}(\pi^{t},P^{t}) , the realized total completion time of schedule π t \pi^{t} , but does not observe the realized job sizes P t P^{t} . For a schedule π \pi , define error ⁡ ( π ) := 𝔼 ​ [ 𝖢𝗈𝗌𝗍 ⁡ ( π , P ) ] − OPT \operatorname{error}(\pi):=\mathbb{E}[\operatorname{{\sf Cost}}(\pi,P)]-\mathrm{OPT} to measure the expected regret we incur by using π \pi for one step.
Recall that in this case an optimal schedule π ∗ \pi^{*} must schedule j j after j ′ j^{\prime} if and only if μ j > μ j ′ \mu_{j}>\mu_{j^{\prime}} .
We say that π \pi inverts a pair of jobs j , j ′ j,j^{\prime} with μ j > μ j ′ \mu_{j}>\mu_{j^{\prime}} if instead it has π ​ ( j ) < π ​ ( j ′ ) \pi(j)<\pi(j^{\prime}) . The following proposition shows that we can write error ⁡ ( π ) \operatorname{error}(\pi) in terms of inversions in π \pi from an optimal schedule. For any schedule π \pi , Let 𝒩 ​ ( π ) = { π ′ ∈ Π n ∣ ∃ i ≠ j , π ​ ( i ) = π ′ ​ ( j ) , π ​ ( j ) = π ′ ​ ( i ) , and ​ π ​ ( k ) = π ′ ​ ( k ) ​ for all ​ k ∉ [ n ] ∖ { i , j } } \mathcal{N}(\pi)=\{\pi^{\prime}\in\Pi_{n}\mid\exists i\neq j,\pi(i)=\pi^{\prime}(j),\pi(j)=\pi^{\prime}(i),\text{ and }\pi(k)=\pi^{\prime}(k)\text{ for all }k\not\in[n]\setminus\{i,j\}\} be the the neighborhood of schedules that consists of swapping a single pair of jobs. We show this admits ( β , γ ) (\beta,\gamma) -improving moves, with γ = 1 + ϵ \gamma=1+\epsilon for any ϵ > 0 \epsilon>0 and β = 1 − ϵ / n 2 . \beta=1-\epsilon/n^{2}. Let π \pi be any schedule with 𝖢𝗈𝗌𝗍 ⁡ ( π ) > ( 1 + ϵ ) ​ OPT \operatorname{{\sf Cost}}(\pi)>(1+\epsilon)\mathrm{OPT} with ϵ ∈ ( 0 , 1 ) \epsilon\in(0,1) , then there exists a schedule π ′ \pi^{\prime} obtainable from π \pi by swapping a single pair of jobs such that First we lower bound 𝖢𝗈𝗌𝗍 ⁡ ( π ) − OPT \operatorname{{\sf Cost}}(\pi)-\mathrm{OPT} by ϵ ⋅ 𝖢𝗈𝗌𝗍 ⁡ ( π ) / 2 \epsilon\cdot\operatorname{{\sf Cost}}(\pi)/2 since Next, we upper bound 𝖢𝗈𝗌𝗍 ⁡ ( π ) − OPT = error ⁡ ( π ) \operatorname{{\sf Cost}}(\pi)-\mathrm{OPT}=\operatorname{error}(\pi) as where we use the fact that there are at most ( n 2 ) \binom{n}{2} terms in the sum.
Combining these bounds, we conclude that there exists a pair j , j ′ j,j^{\prime} with μ j > μ j ′ \mu_{j}>\mu_{j}^{\prime} and π ​ ( j ) < π ​ ( j ′ ) \pi(j)<\pi(j^{\prime}) such that For the new schedule π ′ \pi^{\prime} created by swapping the positions of j j and j ′ j^{\prime} in π \pi , we observe that which uses the fact that π ​ ( j ′ ) − π ​ ( j ) ≥ 1 \pi(j^{\prime})-\pi(j)\geq 1 by construction.
The lemma follows after rearranging.
∎ Consider the problem of scheduling to minimize total completion time with stochastic job sizes drawn from distribution 𝒟 \mathcal{D} . For any ϵ > 0 \epsilon>0 there is an algorithm 𝒜 \mathcal{A} ( Algorithm ˜ 2 ) with We apply Theorem ˜ 3.1 and simplify with problem-specific parameters. For the swap neighborhood, the maximum neighborhood size is M ≤ n 2 M\leq n^{2} . Job sizes are at most 1, so C m ​ a ​ x ≤ n 2 . C_{max}\leq n^{2}. We can bound since log ⁡ ( 1 − x ) ≤ − x \log(1-x)\leq-x for x > − 1 x>-1 . We can also bound since 1 − ϵ / n 2 < 1 , 1-\epsilon/n^{2}<1, implying ( 1 + 1 − ϵ / n 2 ) 4 ≤ 16 (1+\sqrt{1-\epsilon/n^{2}})^{4}\leq 16 . The result follows after substitution and observing log ⁡ n ≤ log ⁡ T \log n\leq\log T .
∎ We now apply our framework to the online stochastic bandit setting for finding a minimum cost base in a matroid.
Let M = ( 𝒮 , ℐ ) M=(\mathcal{S},\mathcal{I}) be a matroid on ground set 𝒮 \mathcal{S} of cardinality | 𝒮 | = n |\mathcal{S}|=n with family of independent sets ℐ \mathcal{I} . Let ℬ \mathcal{B} be its family of bases, or maximal independent sets, so that each that B ∈ ℬ B\in\mathcal{B} satisfies B ∈ ℐ B\in\mathcal{I} and there is no s ∈ S ∖ B s\in S\setminus B such B ∪ s ∈ ℐ B\cup s\in\mathcal{I} . The rank of set S S , denoted r ​ ( S ) r(S) , is the cardinality of the largest independent subset of S S . The rank of matroid M M is the cardinality of a base, so r ​ ( M ) = | B | . r(M)=|B|. In the minimum cost base problem, each element s ∈ 𝒮 s\in\mathcal{S} is associated a cost and the objective is to find a base of minimum total cost.
In the online stochastic bandit setting, the learner acts over a sequence of T T time periods, selecting base B t B^{t} in period t = 1 , … , T t=1,\ldots,T .

sections.heading: 4.1 Stochastic Completion Time Scheduling on a Single Machine
sections.body:
We consider the problem of scheduling stochastic jobs to minimize total completion time on a single machine.
Let 𝒟 \mathcal{D} be a distribution over job sizes which is unknown to the scheduler.
There are n n jobs with stochastic sizes P = ( P 1 , P 2 , … , P n ) ∼ 𝒟 P=(P_{1},P_{2},\ldots,P_{n})\sim\mathcal{D} .
Let μ j := 𝔼 ​ [ P j ] \mu_{j}:=\mathbb{E}[P_{j}] denote the expected size of job j j . We only require independence across time periods; a pair of jobs j j and j ′ j^{\prime} may have correlated size distributions.
We also make a standard assumption that the job size distribution is bounded and normalized to be in [ 0 , 1 ] [0,1] . A schedule is given by a permutation π : [ n ] → [ n ] \pi:[n]\to[n] specifying that job j j is scheduled in position π ​ ( j ) \pi(j) . Let Π n \Pi_{n} be the permutations on [ n ] [n] , the feasible schedules.
Given a schedule π \pi , let C j ​ ( π , P ) := P j + ∑ j ′ : π ​ ( j ′ ) < π ​ ( j ) P j ′ C_{j}(\pi,P):=P_{j}+\sum_{j^{\prime}:\pi(j^{\prime})<\pi(j)}P_{j^{\prime}} be the completion time of job j j under schedule π \pi and sizes P P .
After reordering the summation, the cost of a schedule π \pi is Let OPT := min π ⁡ 𝔼 P ∼ 𝒟 ​ [ 𝖢𝗈𝗌𝗍 ⁡ ( π , P ) ] \mathrm{OPT}:=\min_{\pi}\mathbb{E}_{P\sim\mathcal{D}}[\operatorname{{\sf Cost}}(\pi,P)] .
We focus on learning from limited feedback using fixed, non-preemptive schedules.
In the single machine case full-information case, it is well known that the optimal schedule is given by applying Smith’s rule to the expected job sizes: π ∗ \pi^{*} orders the jobs in non-decreasing order of μ j \mu_{j} , or shortest expected processing time first [ 44 , 46 ] . When learning to schedule with bandit feedback,
the scheduler gains information by interacting with unknown 𝒟 \mathcal{D} through the following process which occurs in each of T T discrete time periods.
In period t ∈ [ T ] t\in[T] , the scheduler commits to a schedule π t \pi^{t} before job sizes are realized according to an independent sample P t ∼ 𝒟 P^{t}\sim\mathcal{D} . Then the scheduler observes 𝖢𝗈𝗌𝗍 ⁡ ( π t , P t ) \operatorname{{\sf Cost}}(\pi^{t},P^{t}) , the realized total completion time of schedule π t \pi^{t} , but does not observe the realized job sizes P t P^{t} . For a schedule π \pi , define error ⁡ ( π ) := 𝔼 ​ [ 𝖢𝗈𝗌𝗍 ⁡ ( π , P ) ] − OPT \operatorname{error}(\pi):=\mathbb{E}[\operatorname{{\sf Cost}}(\pi,P)]-\mathrm{OPT} to measure the expected regret we incur by using π \pi for one step.
Recall that in this case an optimal schedule π ∗ \pi^{*} must schedule j j after j ′ j^{\prime} if and only if μ j > μ j ′ \mu_{j}>\mu_{j^{\prime}} .
We say that π \pi inverts a pair of jobs j , j ′ j,j^{\prime} with μ j > μ j ′ \mu_{j}>\mu_{j^{\prime}} if instead it has π ​ ( j ) < π ​ ( j ′ ) \pi(j)<\pi(j^{\prime}) . The following proposition shows that we can write error ⁡ ( π ) \operatorname{error}(\pi) in terms of inversions in π \pi from an optimal schedule. For any schedule π \pi , Let 𝒩 ​ ( π ) = { π ′ ∈ Π n ∣ ∃ i ≠ j , π ​ ( i ) = π ′ ​ ( j ) , π ​ ( j ) = π ′ ​ ( i ) , and ​ π ​ ( k ) = π ′ ​ ( k ) ​ for all ​ k ∉ [ n ] ∖ { i , j } } \mathcal{N}(\pi)=\{\pi^{\prime}\in\Pi_{n}\mid\exists i\neq j,\pi(i)=\pi^{\prime}(j),\pi(j)=\pi^{\prime}(i),\text{ and }\pi(k)=\pi^{\prime}(k)\text{ for all }k\not\in[n]\setminus\{i,j\}\} be the the neighborhood of schedules that consists of swapping a single pair of jobs. We show this admits ( β , γ ) (\beta,\gamma) -improving moves, with γ = 1 + ϵ \gamma=1+\epsilon for any ϵ > 0 \epsilon>0 and β = 1 − ϵ / n 2 . \beta=1-\epsilon/n^{2}. Let π \pi be any schedule with 𝖢𝗈𝗌𝗍 ⁡ ( π ) > ( 1 + ϵ ) ​ OPT \operatorname{{\sf Cost}}(\pi)>(1+\epsilon)\mathrm{OPT} with ϵ ∈ ( 0 , 1 ) \epsilon\in(0,1) , then there exists a schedule π ′ \pi^{\prime} obtainable from π \pi by swapping a single pair of jobs such that First we lower bound 𝖢𝗈𝗌𝗍 ⁡ ( π ) − OPT \operatorname{{\sf Cost}}(\pi)-\mathrm{OPT} by ϵ ⋅ 𝖢𝗈𝗌𝗍 ⁡ ( π ) / 2 \epsilon\cdot\operatorname{{\sf Cost}}(\pi)/2 since Next, we upper bound 𝖢𝗈𝗌𝗍 ⁡ ( π ) − OPT = error ⁡ ( π ) \operatorname{{\sf Cost}}(\pi)-\mathrm{OPT}=\operatorname{error}(\pi) as where we use the fact that there are at most ( n 2 ) \binom{n}{2} terms in the sum.
Combining these bounds, we conclude that there exists a pair j , j ′ j,j^{\prime} with μ j > μ j ′ \mu_{j}>\mu_{j}^{\prime} and π ​ ( j ) < π ​ ( j ′ ) \pi(j)<\pi(j^{\prime}) such that For the new schedule π ′ \pi^{\prime} created by swapping the positions of j j and j ′ j^{\prime} in π \pi , we observe that which uses the fact that π ​ ( j ′ ) − π ​ ( j ) ≥ 1 \pi(j^{\prime})-\pi(j)\geq 1 by construction.
The lemma follows after rearranging.
∎ Consider the problem of scheduling to minimize total completion time with stochastic job sizes drawn from distribution 𝒟 \mathcal{D} . For any ϵ > 0 \epsilon>0 there is an algorithm 𝒜 \mathcal{A} ( Algorithm ˜ 2 ) with We apply Theorem ˜ 3.1 and simplify with problem-specific parameters. For the swap neighborhood, the maximum neighborhood size is M ≤ n 2 M\leq n^{2} . Job sizes are at most 1, so C m ​ a ​ x ≤ n 2 . C_{max}\leq n^{2}. We can bound since log ⁡ ( 1 − x ) ≤ − x \log(1-x)\leq-x for x > − 1 x>-1 . We can also bound since 1 − ϵ / n 2 < 1 , 1-\epsilon/n^{2}<1, implying ( 1 + 1 − ϵ / n 2 ) 4 ≤ 16 (1+\sqrt{1-\epsilon/n^{2}})^{4}\leq 16 . The result follows after substitution and observing log ⁡ n ≤ log ⁡ T \log n\leq\log T .

Our final application concerns k k -median clustering where the set of points to be clustered are drawn at random from an unknown distribution.
The following setup was studied by Cormode and McGregor [ 17 ] and Guha and Munagala [ 28 ] for a several of k k -clustering objectives in the offline setting.
An instance consists of n n points arriving from a metric space ( ℳ , d ) (\mathcal{M},d) of diameter 1.
The location of the i i ’th point is uncertain and the objective is to choose k k cluster centers which minimize the total expected distance between each arriving point and its closest cluster center.
We let Z i ∈ ℳ Z_{i}\in\mathcal{M} denote the random realization of the i i ’th point. For a fixed location z ∈ ℳ z\in\mathcal{M} and a fixed set C C of cluster centers, let d ​ ( z , C ) = min c ∈ C ⁡ d ​ ( z , c ) d(z,C)=\min_{c\in C}d(z,c) denote the distance of z z to its nearest cluster center in C C .
Then the cost of a solution C C on points Z = ( Z 1 , Z 2 , … , Z n ) Z=(Z_{1},Z_{2},\ldots,Z_{n}) is Thus, the expected cost of solution C C is given by and OPT \mathrm{OPT} denotes the expected cost of a set of cluster centers which minimizes this expected cost. In the bandit setting, we do not observe the realizations of the point locations. In each round t t a set of cluster center locations C t C_{t} is submitted, after which the learner only observes 𝖢𝗈𝗌𝗍 ⁡ ( C t , Z ) \operatorname{{\sf Cost}}(C_{t},Z) . Arya et. al. [ 3 ] propose a local search algorithm for the k k -medians problem which starts from an arbitrary set of k k cluster centers C C and repeatedly moves to neighboring C ′ ∈ 𝒩 ​ ( C ) C^{\prime}\in\mathcal{N}(C) satisfying 𝖢𝗈𝗌𝗍 ⁡ ( C ′ ) ≤ ( 1 − ϵ ) ​ 𝖢𝗈𝗌𝗍 ⁡ ( C ) \operatorname{{\sf Cost}}(C^{\prime})\leq(1-\epsilon)\operatorname{{\sf Cost}}(C) until no local improvement is found. Cormode and McGregor [ 17 ] shows that this is equally effective for the uncertain k k -medians problem. For the uncertain k k -median clustering problem, local search with ϵ \epsilon -improvements terminates in C ∈ 𝒳 C\in\mathcal{X} satisfying 𝖢𝗈𝗌𝗍 ⁡ ( C ) ≤ ( 5 / ( 1 − n ​ ϵ ) ) ​ 𝖢𝗈𝗌𝗍 ⁡ ( C ∗ ) . \operatorname{{\sf Cost}}(C)\leq(5/(1-n\epsilon))\operatorname{{\sf Cost}}(C^{*}). We can leverage this to show that uncertain k k -median clustering permits ( β , γ ) (\beta,\gamma) -improving moves. The Minimum Cost K K -Median problem with n n input points admits ( β , γ ) (\beta,\gamma) -improving moves where γ = 5 / ( 1 − 1 n ) \gamma=5/(1-\frac{1}{n}) and β = ( 1 − 1 / n 2 ) \beta=(1-{1}/{n^{2}}) . We prove this lemma by contradiction. Suppose that the local search algorithm does not admit ( β , γ ) (\beta,\gamma) -improving moves where γ = 5 / ( 1 − 1 n ) \gamma=5/(1-\frac{1}{n}) and β = ( 1 − 1 / n 2 ) \beta=(1-{1}/{n^{2}}) . This implies that there exists C ∈ 𝒳 C\in\mathcal{X} with 𝖢𝗈𝗌𝗍 ⁡ ( C ) > γ ​ 𝖢𝗈𝗌𝗍 ⁡ ( C ∗ ) \operatorname{{\sf Cost}}(C)>\gamma\operatorname{{\sf Cost}}(C^{*}) and that 𝖢𝗈𝗌𝗍 ⁡ ( C ′ ) > β ​ 𝖢𝗈𝗌𝗍 ⁡ ( C ) \operatorname{{\sf Cost}}(C^{\prime})>\beta\operatorname{{\sf Cost}}(C) for all C ′ ∈ 𝒩 ​ ( C ) C^{\prime}\in\mathcal{N}(C) .
It follows that the local search algorithm of [ 17 ] may terminate with C C as a local optimum.
However, setting ϵ = 1 / n 2 \epsilon=1/n^{2} in Lemma ˜ 4.9 gives a 5 / ( 1 − 1 / n ) 5/(1-1/n) -factor approximation algorithm.
This implies that 𝖢𝗈𝗌𝗍 ⁡ ( C ) ≤ 5 1 − 1 n ​ 𝖢𝗈𝗌𝗍 ⁡ ( C ∗ ) \operatorname{{\sf Cost}}(C)\leq\frac{5}{1-\frac{1}{n}}\operatorname{{\sf Cost}}(C^{*}) , a contradiction. ∎ Consider the uncertain k k -median clustering with candidate set of m m potential cluster centers where the locations of n n points in a metric space of diameter 1 are drawn from distribution 𝒟 \mathcal{D} . There exists algorithm 𝒜 \mathcal{A} ( Algorithm ˜ 2 ) achieving when m 2 ≤ T m^{2}\leq T . We apply Theorem ˜ 3.1 and simplify with problem-specific parameters. For the swap neighborhood, the maximum neighborhood size is M ≤ | 𝒳 | 2 = m 2 M\leq|\mathcal{X}|^{2}=m^{2} . The metric space has diameter 1, so C m ​ a ​ x ≤ n . C_{max}\leq n. As in the proof of Corollary ˜ 4.3 , We can bound log ⁡ ( 1 / α ) ≥ 1 / 2 ​ n 2 \log(1/\alpha)\geq 1/{2n^{2}} and δ 2 ​ β ≥ 8 / n 4 \delta^{2}\beta\geq 8/n^{4} . sections.heading: 5 Discussion
sections.body:
We conclude by discussing a couple of relevant issues not raised elsewhere. For ease of exposition we assumed the algorithm knows T T , and that there is an upper bound C max C_{\max} on costs.
It is straightforward but tedious to modify the algorithms and analysis to not require explicit knowledge of T T .
Similarly, we can replace the assumption of bounded costs with suitable distributional assumptions about the cost.
For example, we could require a sub-gaussian like condition on the tails of the distribution or even just finite variance.
In the latter case, we could make use of tools for handling heavy-tailed distributions in the bandit setting [ 11 ] . It remains to computationally demonstrate the benefits of our algorithm.
All three applications are instances of linear bandits [ 19 , 37 , 10 ] , so a natural starting point may be to compare the regret of our algorithm with that of a linear bandit style algorithm which, in each step, uses the bandit feedback to estimate problem parameters (eg. mean job sizes for completion time scheduling), then computes the solution to an offline instance of the problem with those parameters to submit in the next time step. For the scheduling and matroid applications the offline instances can be solved efficiently, for clustering we have to resort to an approximate solution due to NP-hardness. An exciting direction for future work is exploring further applications for our framework where local search is effective offline.
Some applications may require generalizing our current approach, for example, the local search guarantees for set cover [ 29 ] rely on finding neighboring solution which improves a specialized potential function, and not the objective function directly.
It is an open question whether a version of ( β , γ ) (\beta,\gamma) -improving moves defined on such potential functions can provide guarantees in the bandit online setting.

title: NOMAD: Generating Embeddings for Massive Distributed Graphs
authors: Aishwarya Sarkar1, Sayan Ghosh2, Nathan R. Tallent2, Ali Jannesari1
abstract: Abstract Successful machine learning on graphs or networks requires embeddings that not only represent nodes and edges as low-dimensional vectors but also preserve the graph structure. Established methods for generating embeddings require flexible exploration of the entire graph through repeated use of random walks that capture graph structure with samples of nodes and edges. These methods create scalability challenges for massive graphs with millions-to-billions of edges because single-node solutions have inadequate memory and processing capabilities. We present Nomad , a distributed-memory graph embedding framework using the Message Passing Interface (MPI) for distributed graphs. Nomad implements proximity-based models proposed in the widely popular LINE (Large-scale Information Network Embedding) algorithm. We propose several practical trade-offs to improve the scalability and communication overheads confronted by irregular and distributed graph embedding methods, catering to massive-scale graphs arising in web and science domains. Nomad demonstrates median speedups of 10/100 × \times on CPU-based NERSC Perlmutter cluster relative to the popular reference implementations of multi-threaded LINE and node2vec, 35–76 × \times over distributed PBG, and competitive embedding quality relative to LINE, node2vec, and GraphVite, while yielding 12–370 × \times end-to-end speedups on real-world graphs.

sections.heading: I Introduction
sections.body:
Graphs are ubiquitous in modeling complex systems and representing entity interactions to uncover a domain’s structural information.
Graph representation learning broadly spans shallow transductive methods (proximity-based) that learn directly from graph structure and inductive message-passing models (graph neural networks (GNNs)) that additionally rely on node features and supervision (labels) [ 9 , 10 ] . Proximity-based methods such as LINE [ 11 ] , DeepWalk [ 12 ] , and node2vec [ 13 ] belong to the former class, learning node representations from sampled pairs derived from edge lists or breadth/depth-first traversals or that generate random sequences from a probability distribution. Walk-based methods such as DeepWalk and node2vec derive training pairs from multi-hop co-occurrence context, whereas LINE directly optimizes sampled proximity pairs (faster on massive graphs).
For many real-world large scientific and web graphs, attributes and labels may be incomplete, noisy, heterogeneous, or unavailable making GNN training less effective or harder to apply directly [ 14 , 15 , 16 , 17 , 18 ] . In such cases, structure-only methods are a more practical choice for unsupervised learning and can also provide useful initial node representations for downstream supervised GNNs, when informative node features are missing/unreliable. Traditional dimensionality-reduction approaches are typically quadratic in the number of vertices and therefore intractable for medium-to-large graphs [ 19 , 20 , 21 , 22 ] . For large graphs with millions to billions of nodes and edges, such as the web-scale and scientific graphs found in biology and chemistry, embedding generation becomes a major systems bottleneck due to irregular memory access, limited locality, and high communication overhead. This cost stems from repeated random-walk exploration and training on the resulting sampled pairs, which expose irregular communication and load imbalance at scale. Random walks remain a standard mechanism for extracting structural information for graph embedding, irrespective of specific walk biases used to generate the node sequences [ 23 ] . However, although state-of-the-art shallow embedding systems exploit multithreading and other within-node optimizations, they remain constrained by node memory capacity, and methods that preserve global network properties often require synchronization patterns that do not scale well across distributed memory [ 24 , 3 ] . Prior efforts on distributed graph embeddings either focus on accelerating a part of the pipeline (like random walks [ 25 ] ) or rely on parameter-server architecture (to manage and synchronize shared model parameters) [ 1 ] with limited scalability potential across nodes. Parallelism based on graph partitions alone cannot scale this problem without specific interventions since inherent irregularities in the graph partitions cause load imbalance (partitioning is NP-hard [ 26 ] ). At scale, graph-induced access patterns require both memory capacity and sufficient compute concurrency, making single-node scale-up approaches insufficient, necessitating distributed scale-out over partitioned graphs. Capacity alone is not enough: without proportional bandwidth and compute, disaggregated or pooled memory cannot effectively sustain embedding generation for these irregular workloads [ 27 ] . Table I summarizes the design space of representative graph embedding systems [ 1 , 2 , 3 , 4 , 5 , 6 , 7 , 8 ] across relevant dimensions, including hardware platform, end-to-end sampling and training support, and scale-up/scale-out capabilities.
Single-node CPU/GPU based embedding generation can drive node throughput through optimized memory layouts, concurrency, host-device overlap, and cache-efficient sampling [ 4 , 3 ] . But such optimizations fall apart on distributed-memory considering partitioned graphs, which must reconcile with irregular data intensive graph exploration (either through structure-preserving random walks or weighted edge sampling), coordination with training and managing synchronization overheads.
Consequently, existing distributed GPU approaches consider either sampling or training, but not both as part of the same system. We propose Nomad , a distributed-memory scalable
proximity-based end-to-end graph embedding generator (that learns from sampled positive/negative pairs similar to LINE [ 11 ] , DeepWalk [ 12 ] , and node2vec [ 13 ] ). Nomad proposes several communication-avoiding and synchronization trade-offs while preserving acceptable embedding accuracy.
For wide portability, Nomad uses MPI collectives to efficiently handle non-uniform communication volumes across non-uniform process sets.
Our contributions are: 1. Distributed-memory implementation of the popular second-order proximity model of LINE. Distributed-memory implementation of the popular second-order proximity model of LINE. 2. Several performance/quality trade-offs for distributed random walk exploration that enable Nomad to enhance communication/synchronization efficiency. Several performance/quality trade-offs for distributed random walk exploration that enable Nomad to enhance communication/synchronization efficiency. 3. Detailed quality and performance evaluation on NERSC Perlmutter across diverse large-scale graphs, showing up to 370 × 370\times speedup over strong single-node baselines and 35 35 – 76 × 76\times speedup over distributed-memory baselines, and competitive embedding quality. Detailed quality and performance evaluation on NERSC Perlmutter across diverse large-scale graphs, showing up to 370 × 370\times speedup over strong single-node baselines and 35 35 – 76 × 76\times speedup over distributed-memory baselines, and competitive embedding quality. The paper is organized as follows: § II reviews proximity-based embedding and distributed tradeoffs, § III discusses related work, § IV presents Nomad ’s distributed methodology, and § V reports quality and performance evaluations.

sections.heading: II Background
sections.body:
Proximity-based embedding models learn low-dimensional vertex representations by optimizing over large collections of independently sampled training pairs of vertices of an arbitrary graph G = ( V , E ) G=(V,E) , treating these pairs as the fundamental unit of parallelism. A positive pair ( u , v ) : G = ( V , E ) s . t . { u , v } ∈ E ∣ u ∈ V a n d v ∈ V (u,v):G=(V,E)\;s.t.\;\{u,v\}\in E\mid u\in V\;and\;v\in V denotes two vertices that are structurally similar (or, closer), typically extracted from local graph neighborhoods or co-occurrence within short random walks, while a negative pair ( u , v − ) : G = ( V , E ) s . t . { u , v − } ∉ E ∣ u ∈ V a n d v − ∈ V (u,v^{-}):G=(V,E)\;s.t.\;\{u,v^{-}\}\notin E\mid u\in V\;and\;v^{-}\in V puts the target vertex u u against a randomly sampled vertex v − v^{-} that does not appear in the same context, providing a stabilizing signal during training [ 28 ] . Each node is associated with two low-dimensional vectors, a vertex embedding U U and a context embedding C C , whose dot product determines similarity.
At scale, embedding computation itself is lightweight, consisting primarily of vector dot products and updates. DeepWalk and node2vec treat random walks as “sentences” and optimize skip-gram-style objectives over the resulting co-occurrences, while LINE trains on edge- or proximity-derived pairs with similar updates [ 29 , 11 ] .
